title: Adjacent Event: Snack Break - Explore AI Accountability Investigations with the Pulitzer Center
description_clean: What are the ways AI may intersect with your reporting, whether it be algorithmic influence on government decisions, environmental impact, or simply lots of hype around time and cost savings? How might the Pulitzer Center’s training and funding opportunities support your dream reporting project connected to the accountability of AI systems and companies? Join Pulitzer Center’s Executive Editor Marina Walker Guevara and Steve Sapienza, Senior Editor, U.S. News Partnerships for networking and a casual discussion exploring these questions. Coffee and light snacks will be provided!

title: IRE Board Meeting
description_clean: Open to all IRE members.

title: Navigating risk: Practical safety for journalists reporting in the U.S.
speakers: Jennifer Nelson; Katherine Jacobsen; Ximena Natera
speaker_org_titles: Committee to Protect Journalists | Freelance visual journalist  | Reporters Committee for Freedom of the Press
description_clean: Reporting in the field increasingly exposes journalists to complex safety challenges—from covering protests to navigating surveillance technologies and law enforcement encounters in cities like Los Angeles, Chicago, Minneapolis, and beyond. Recent conversations among journalists have underscored urgent concerns, including how to safely navigate interactions with ICE and how to distinguish between different law enforcement agencies on the ground. Join the International Women’s Media Foundation (IWMF) and the Journalist Assistance Network for a practical session focused on assessing and managing these risks. Safety experts will share strategies for mitigating on-the-ground threats, along with practical guidance for covering protests and high-risk situations. Attendees will leave with concrete, actionable tools to help them report safely in volatile environments—so they can document critical stories without compromising their personal security.

title: Get that TPS report (with cover sheet): Easy, no-code AI document finders
speaker_org_titles: Brechner FOI Project, University of Florida | MuckRock, CTO
description_clean: This hands-on training session shows how easy it is to use AI to find specific documents relevant to your investigation without needing programming skills, money or sign-on accounts. You will find and download a public records request log (or bring your own as Excel/PDF files - requested ahead of time from your local agency) and run it through NotebookLM, comparing results to a Google search. We will also demonstrate other useful document finder tools, including the fed’s FOIA Wizard, MuckRock’s FOIA Explorer, FOIAengine (free to IRE members), and new state-level tools being developed by MuckRock. Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

title: Google Sheets 1: Getting started with spreadsheets
speaker_org_titles: KFF Health News
description_clean: In this introduction to spreadsheets, you'll begin analyzing data with Google Sheets, a simple but powerful tool. You'll learn how to enter data, navigate spreadsheets and conduct simple calculations like sum, average and median. This session is good for: Data beginners. You must bring your own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class, and you will need a free Google account to participate.

title: Crafting impactful digital video investigations
speakers: Courtney Kan; Jarrett Ley; Mark Scheffler
speaker_org_titles: Former Senior Editor for Visuals, The Washington Post | Head of Visual Investigations | New York Times | Visual Forensics Reporter, The Washington Post
description_clean: In this session, reporters and editors will share how they’re incorporating video and visual evidence into investigations for newspapers (not broadcast). Learn how they use bystander video, satellite imagery and other open-source material in a variety of digital formats to drive accountability reporting — and how they guide these productions across a large cross-functional newsroom.

title: The latest intel on big tech and AI threats, and tools to expose them
speakers: Andrea Arzaba; Craig Silverman; Lam Thuy Vo
speaker_org_titles: Associate Professor & Investigative Reporter, CUNY & Documented | GIJN | Indicator
description_clean: GIJN recently produced best-practices stories, videos, a report, and a webinar for its Tech Focus Project based on the knowledge and exchange of ideas of 100 experts in the field. This session will distill the highlights: tools for detecting AI-generated content; experimenting with AI tools; cutting-edge tips to protect from surveillance; tools to investigate government use of AI, and techniques for radical collaboration to counter Big Tech’s assault on the info ecosystem. Andrea Arzaba (GIJN), Craig Silverman (Indicator), Lam Thuy Vo (Documented/CUNY/Pulitzer Center), and Marina Walker Guevara (Pulitzer Center) will share tips and experiences around investigating Tech and using Tech for investigations.

title: Data deluge: The education beat awaits your investigation
speakers: Aliyya Swaby; Beth Hawkins; Marta Jewson
speaker_org_titles: Independent Journalist at lamplighter.news | ProPublica reporter | The 74 Million
description_clean: Education is more than test scores, spelling bees and principal appointments. One of the largest line items in state budgets, the education beat has a trove of data at the local, state and federal level — from suspension and seclusion rates, demographic data and funding disparities, to civil rights challenges and special education settlements with NDAs. There is a story in your school district — you just have to find it. Education stories are easily accessible if you know the tools.

title: Concentrated power: the growing corporate takeover
speakers: Arlene Martinez; Evan Simon; Mike Morris
speaker_org_titles: Good Jobs First | Houston Chronicle
description_clean: Companies are getting bigger, more consolidated, and increasingly powerful, instrumental in changing policies impacting every aspect of our lives. During this panel, learn how to probe more deeply into corporate actions and relevant relationships, learn about dark money-backed plans at the state level for 2026 and beyond, and walk away with new tools to better investigate company behavior, even on deadline.

title: Video show & tell: Anything goes with Laura Geller and Matt Goldberg
speaker_org_titles: CBS News Investigative Unit | VP & News Director, CBS LA
description_clean: Sign up to “Show & Tell” us about your best work and get constructive feedback from the moderator and audience. Sign-ups are required for presenters. Each presenter will have 15 minutes to share their work and receive feedback. All are welcome to attend! Sign up here: https://www.signupgenius.com/go/10C084CA8AC29A4FBCE9-64417636-video *These sessions will not be recorded.

title: Deep dive: Two podcasters talk about telling the unforgettable story
speaker_org_titles: Independent journalist | Serial Productions
description_clean: Julie Snyder and Dan Taberski are two of the country’s best-known podcasters. Snyder co-founded Serial, and Serial invented a form, launching the narrative podcast boom. Serial hit the country like a thunderclap. Taberski has made so many poignant , mind-bending podcasts (“Missing Richard Simmons,” “The Line,” “9/12,” “Hysterical”) that The New Yorker has turned his name into an adjective: “Taberskian.” They’ll talk about how to tell a story that no one will forget.

title: Everything you want to know about tracking political influence and money in politics
speakers: Anna Massoglia; Robert Maguire; Soorin Kim
speaker_org_titles: Citizens for Responsibility and Ethics in Washington (CREW) | MuckRock, Editor | White House producer at MS NOW
description_clean: From dark money networks and corporate influence campaigns to billionaire mega-donors and grassroots fundraising, money in politics touches almost every beat. Panelists with decades of combined experience tracking campaign finances, nonprofit spending and political influence walk through how they actually find the information that drives these stories. Journalists will learn how to piece together opaque spending networks and uncover crucial details about operations through tax returns, digital ad data, corporate records, lobbying disclosures and other sources.

title: Backgrounding people in an hour
speaker_org_titles: Global Investigative Reporter, The Associated Press
description_clean: Whether covering a mass shooting or a political scandal, reporters need to know how to thoroughly and quickly background people and companies involved in breaking news events and long-term investigations. In this panel, journalists will share concrete tips on how to mine social media accounts, maximize Nexis searches, unearth public records, find friends and relatives and turn hunches into distinctive stories.

title: Outlining and structure: The writer's missing manual
speaker_org_titles: New York Times
description_clean: Outlining might be the single best thing you can do to make you a better, faster writer. This isn't the roman numeral outlining your middle school teacher taught you. We'll take a story from the ground up, showing how early considerations about structure and framing develop into a draft. Preregistration is required and seating is limited.

title: 15 min break
Networking: Educators
description_clean: Mix and mingle with peers from throughout the world to find solidarity, make new friends, and build your professional community in this fun and informal networking session. This session is for those who teach journalism.

title: No code, open data: Using immigration data without coding
speaker_org_titles: University of Maryland
description_clean: Don’t know coding? No fear, this session will teach attendees how they can use data from the Deportation Data Project without any coding. Using Google Sheets, attendees will learn how they can perform analysis on publicly available immigration data. The session will demystify key fields in the data, provide practical definitions and identify pitfalls to avoid. Reporters will gain the foundation to find stories related to their communities in this trove of data. Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

title: Finding the story: Court records
speaker_org_titles: Senior Reporter, Law360 and Contributor to Institute for Public Service Reporting / WKNO Radio
description_clean: In this interactive hands-on session, bring your laptop and practice with us as we show you new tools to help you analyze complex court records - especially on deadline. This hands-on session focuses primarily on two powerful analysis tools: Google Pinpoint and Draftable. We’ll also briefly discuss PACER and RECAP, bread-and-butter tools to help you locate court records across the country. We’ll keep this session lively with fun challenges, so come hang out with us! This session is good for: everyone! No previous data experience is required. Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

title: Google Sheets 2: Formulas & sorting
speaker_org_titles: Data Editor, Howard Center for Investigative Journalism at Arizona State University
description_clean: Much of Google Sheets' power comes in the form of formulas. In this class, you'll learn how to use them to analyze data with the eye of a journalist. Yes, math will be involved, but it's totally worth it! This class will show you how calculations like change, percent change, rates and ratios can beef up your reporting. This session is good for: Anyone who has taken Google Sheets 1 or has been introduced to spreadsheets. You must bring your own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class, and you will need a free Google account to participate.

title: Breaking news! What now?
speaker_org_titles: Investigative Reporter WABC-TV New York | Investigative Reporter, WRC TV NBC Washington
description_clean: It’s not breaking news that a strong I-Team should be on the air when major news breaks but too often, investigative reporters wait to be invited in. Don’t! We will show why & exactly how to add value in minutes. You’ll leave with a practical checklist for what to do now, what to do the moment news breaks, what to do before the first day ends, how to follow up the next day, and how to turn a chaotic breaking story into a sharper investigative piece the next week or month. Bring your breaking-news confusion; leave with a playbook.

title: Covering AI’s environmental impact: How to make a complex story accessible
speaker_org_titles: Assistant Director, Trusting News
description_clean: Artificial intelligence is rapidly reshaping the world—but its environmental impact is still poorly understood by many audiences. Data centers consume enormous amounts of energy and water, and AI expansion is raising new questions about climate and resource use. Yet much of the reporting on this topic is highly technical and difficult for audiences to engage with. This panel will explore how journalists can better cover AI’s environmental footprint in ways that are clear, compelling, and accessible. Research from Trusting News shows that many people care deeply about the environmental impact of emerging technologies—but often struggle to understand coverage or see how it connects to their lives. Panelists will share examples of reporting approaches that help audiences get “on-ramps” into this complex issue. These include social media explainers, visual storytelling, social cards, and narrative framing that makes the stakes understandable without oversimplifying the science or policy. The discussion will focus on practical strategies reporters and editors can use to translate complicated environmental and technology reporting into stories that invite audiences in—and motivate deeper engagement.

title: Funding your dream investigation project
speakers: Eric Ferrero; Jacqueline Stenson; Jane Sasseen; Jill Blackman; Steve Sapienza
speaker_org_titles: Executive Director, Fund for Investigative Journalism | McGraw Center for Business Journalism  | Pulitzer Center | USC Annenberg Center for Health Journalism
description_clean: With budget cuts and layoffs, more staff reporters and independent journalists must now fund their own investigative reporting projects. Fortunately, organizations like the Fund for Investigative Journalism, Pulitzer Center, and Data-Driven Reporting Project offer financial support. This workshop will help IRE members connect with funders, learn about resources for freelancers, and get guidance on finding funding, securing publisher commitments, and drafting strong grant pitches. We’ll cover key elements of a successful pitch, budgeting basics, and strategies to catch editors’ and grant evaluators’ attention.

title: Driveway moments: Crafting stories that stop listeners in their tracks
speakers: Alison MacAdam; Howard Berkes; Nadia Hamdan; Sukey Lewis
speaker_org_titles: Freelance story editor | KQED | Retired Correspondent, NPR Investigations; Chair, Governing Board, Public Health Watch | Reveal
description_clean: Investigative journalism meets powerful storytelling in this panel designed for audio creators aiming to create those unforgettable "driveway moments"—when listeners can’t turn off the story even after arriving at their destination. Panelists will explore how to build suspense, evoke emotion, and connect with audiences on a deeply personal level while staying true to the investigative rigor. From scene-setting to pacing, attendees will gain practical strategies for turning complex investigations into gripping, memorable audio experiences.

title: What do YOU do when diversity, equity & inclusion becomes taboo?
speaker_org_titles: 13 Investigates Reporter, WTHR | Independent Journalist, editor & forthcoming book author
description_clean: How do you produce inclusive and compelling storytelling on the important stories in your community if your newsroom priorities have changed? We’ll lead an honest conversation about how the shift away from diversity, equity and inclusion impacts our newsrooms, us as journalists and our journalism. The discussion will explore strategies on how to navigate corporate buzzwords and move towards editorial impact, reader trust, and community engagement.

title: Unsung documents
speaker_org_titles: Investigative Correspondent, NPR | Investigative Journalist
description_clean: Veteran investigative reporters reprise a classic panel and list obscure but powerful documents that anyone can use to turbocharge a project or bulk-up daily beat coverage. They also provide practical guidelines for unearthing, scrutinizing, managing and cross-referencing routine documents to unleash their full investigative power.

title: Investigating Trump
speakers: Eric Lipton; Hannah Natanson; Josh Dawsey
speaker_org_titles: Narrative enterprise reporter, The Washington Post | Reporter, WSJ | The New York Times
description_clean: With the curtailment of oversight by the Justice Department, federal inspectors general, the Office of Government Ethics, Office of Special Counsel and Republicans in Congress, the news media has become one of the only remaining entities capable to examining actions by President Trump, his family, donors and his cabinet officials to at least bring some transparency to wrongdoing. Reporters from The NYT, WSJ, Bloomberg, Reuters, ProPublica, The WashPost, NPR, among others, have been doing this work. But it is something that reporters from around the U.S. and the world can help with. We talk about how to take up such an effort, examining some examples from the last 18 months. But we also spend time considering how various news entities in spots across the nation and the world can take up this challenge. Accountability still matters.

title: 15 min break
Networking: Freelancers
description_clean: Mix and mingle, meet friends old and new, and build your professional community in this fun and informal networking session. This session is for people who work as freelancers.

tracks: Public records; Reporting & writing strategies; Research; Story ideas
title: Full disclosure: Using state ethics disclosures to power accountability reporting
speakers: Audrey Nielsen; Diara J. Townes; Michael Nolan
speaker_org_titles: Engagement Journalist, NC Local, The Margin | Investigative and Research Journalist, MuckRock | Investigative Journalist and Researcher
description_clean: Public officials and candidates across the country file personal financial and economic interest disclosures, and those records can be a goldmine for accountability reporting, useful for surfacing conflicts of interest, tracking business entanglements, backgrounding candidates, and connecting the dots between officials' financial lives and their public decisions. But the data is scattered across fifty different systems with different rules, formats, and levels of accessibility. In this session, we’ll dig into the why, how states compare, what’s available, and how you can use disclosure data for your own work. This session will walk through how to find, access, and use disclosure data to support reporting on elections, real estate, inter-and-intra-state business connections, environmental policy, and more. We'll highlight examples of disclosure-driven reporting from across the country, and we'll demo a growing collaborative database of disclosures collected from states nationwide as part of an ongoing open project that attendees are invited to join. We'll also share a comprehensive tipsheet on what data is available in each state and run through strategies for building a continuous workflow around these using these filings on your beat. Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

title: What crime stats can (and can't) tell you
speaker_org_titles: Director, The Center for Just Journalism
description_clean: Crime statistics generate a lot of media coverage, but they can be hard to interpret and often obscure broader public safety trends. This workshop will offer practical recommendations for analyzing traditional crime data using locational and historical context, rather than chasing year-over-year fluctuations. It will also include tips for accessing data on other public safety issues, like housing, workplace, and environmental safety. Through real-world examples, hands-on exercises, and guided practice, journalists will leave with concrete tools to cover public safety data with more depth and nuance. Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

title: Google Sheets 3: Filtering & pivot tables
description_clean: A look at the awesome power of pivot — and how to use it to analyze your dataset in minutes rather than hours. We'll work up to using a pivot table by first sorting and filtering a dataset, learning how to find story ideas along the way. This session is good for: Anyone familiar with formulas, sorting and filtering in a spreadsheet program. You must bring your own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class, and you will need a free Google account to participate.

title: How they attack us: A field guide to understanding and responding to anti-investigative propaganda
speakers: Bret Schafer; Drew Sullivan; Martha Mendoza
speaker_org_titles: Co-Founder and Publisher, OCCRP | FRONTLINE/PBS | ISD-US
description_clean: Investigative reporting creates enemies—that is a certainty. While physical threats and legal harassment are serious dangers, the most frequent global threat is sophisticated disinformation aimed at discrediting the facts or the journalists. Drawing on years of experience observing real-time campaigns launched by state media, government officials, and anti-establishment influencers following major exposés, this session will provide a practical, action-oriented playbook for newsrooms under attack. Revealing the highly predictable narratives used to undermine accountability journalism, attendees will learn how to effectively counter disinformation and develop proactive strategies to pre-bunk and inoculate your audience against predictable attacks.

title: Deep dive: Turning investigative reporting into the stuff of literature
speaker_org_titles: The Atlantic
description_clean: Pulitzer Prize winners Caitlin Dickerson and Stephanie McCrummen both write for The Atlantic, and when you see their bylines, you know to read. Dickerson writes epic pieces steeped in horror, from “Seventy Miles in Hell,” on the Darién Gap, to the secret history of the U.S. government’s family-separation policy. McCrummen goes where few reporters venture and writes opening sentences like this: “On the day she heard God tell her to buy a mountain, Tami Barthen already sensed that her life was on a spiritual upswing.” Their talk will take you to the intersection of investigative journalism and narrative.

title: Opening Reception Sponsored by NBC News and NBCUniversal Local
description_clean: The reception sponsor, NBC News, will offer a few words before IRE's former executive director, Diana Fuentes, is recognized. Light bites will be available along with two drink ticket for each person. Conference badges are required for admittance.

title: Recharge Room
description_clean: Need a spot to decompress, take a load off, get your bearings or recharge your batteries? This is the room for you!

title: Game Night
description_clean: Have some fun with friends old and new while playing board games, cards, etc. Games and cards are provided, and feel free to bring your own. All conference attendees are welcome.

title: Registration Hours
description_clean: Pick up your badge or register on-site: 7:30 a.m. - 6:30 p.m.

title: Adjacent Event: Journalists Recovery Network
description_clean: The Journalists Recovery Network will meet and offer a sober space for journalists experiencing troubles with alcohol or substance use. This is an informal meeting for individuals who may struggle with substance use to meet our members and chat more about our organization. Feel free to reach out to us at journalistsrecoverynetwork@gmail.com.

title: Welcome to IRE26 + first-timers networking
speakers: Francisco Vara-Orta; Josh Hinkle; Lauren Grandestaff
speaker_org_titles: Director of Investigations & Innovation - KXAN | Investigative Reporters & Editors | IRE & NICAR
description_clean: Welcome to the conference! Hear about tips and tactics to navigate our conference like a pro. Then, stick around for a speed round of networking with fellow attendees and IRE staff and board.

title: Exhibitors
description_clean: Stop by and visit with the exhibitors and recruiters.

title: Audio Coaching 1:1
description_clean: Sign up for 30 minutes with an audio pro to use however you like: Bring tape for a critique, a pitch for workshopping or your questions about surviving and thriving in the audio journalism space.

title: Barry Nash and Company One-on-One Coaching
description_clean: Barry Nash and his team return to IRE to provide personalized coaching sessions. If you have questions about any aspect of your delivery — your appearance, how you use your voice, how you can perform most effectively in the field or in the studio — this is an opportunity you won’t want to miss. Barry has been working with talent at all levels of experience and ability for over 30 years. He currently authors the only blog in the world focused exclusively on television news and sports performance. You can find it on Substack Please note: Participants should bring recent footage on a flash drive, webpage/online link, or storage drive (Google or Dropbox). Sign up for a time slot here: https://www.signupgenius.com/go/10C0445A9AD28A5FEC34-64383836-irecoaching#/

title: Data visualization for non-designers
speaker_org_titles: Capital News Service Data and Graphics Bureau / University of Maryland
description_clean: There are many tools developed in recent years that allow anyone with a basic grasp of spreadsheets and a browser to make beautiful and publication-ready data visualizations. This session will show attendees how to create storytelling infographics and visualizations for their stories using off-the-shelf tools like Datawrapper, Flourish Studio and others. Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

tracks: Data analysis; Hands on; Tools & tech
title: Intro to data mapping: Getting started with QGIS
speaker_org_titles: Mongabay
description_clean: Learn the basics of mapping analysis with examples from award-winning journalist and educator Andy Lehren. Leave with ideas to hit the ground running in your own newsroom. Data track; bring your own laptop. Seated is limited; laptop prep is required. Please fill out this form to get the necessary information. Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

title: From Boring to blockbuster: Tricks and tools to up your visual game
speakers: Josh Hinkle; Mike Schuh; Nadia Sussman; Owen Hornstein III
speaker_org_titles: Director of Investigations & Innovation - KXAN | Director of Photography & VFX - InvestigateTV | MS StoryWorks/NPPA | Video Journalist, ProPublica
description_clean: When doing a story with hard facts and heavy data, brining an investigative story to life can sometimes be a challenge. A challenge yes...but not impossible! In this session, we'll share how you can make a non-visual story come to life. From framing interviews to color grading, graphics, and editing. Photojournalists, including NPPA leaders, will share how you can level up your visual game in an interesting, different, and creative way.

title: Front-line leadership: How newsrooms manage risk, safety, and law enforcement coverage
speakers: Angelica Mayor; April Alonso; David Armiak; Elizabeth Dunbar; Ximena Natera
speaker_org_titles: Digital Editor |CoFounder - Cicero Independiente | Executive Editor, MinnPost | Freelance visual journalist  | Program Manager, International Women's Media Foundation | Research Director and Investigative Journalist, Center for Media and Democracy
description_clean: Newsroom leaders from cities experiencing heightened law-enforcement activity will discuss how they are assessing risk and fulfilling their responsibility to protect journalists in the field. Drawing on real newsroom decision-making, panelists will explore how they prepare reporters for volatile assignments, establish safety protocols, respond to emergencies, and balance editorial imperatives with physical and legal risk. They’ll also discuss internal communication, legal coordination, community relationships, and how leadership decisions before and during an assignment directly affect reporter safety. This moderated discussion, led by the International Women’s Media Foundation (IWMF), will offer practical guidance for editors and managers navigating unpredictable reporting conditions — with candid reflections on what has worked, what hasn’t, and what newsrooms must do differently going forward.

title: Let’s get personal: Turning congressional financial disclosures into exclusive  stories
speakers: Bryan Metzger; Dave Levinthal; Taylor Giorno
speaker_org_titles: Former Senior Politics Reporter at Business Insider | Reporter at NOTUS covering all things money in politics. | Senior editor at NOTUS overseeing money and politics, investigations and special projects.
description_clean: Congressional lawmakers and candidates are required to file annual financial disclosures and report their stock transactions. These disclosures are a wealth of stories, as the speakers on this panel have proven. Attendees can expect to learn how to access financial disclosures, how to read them and how to use them to craft compelling articles that break news. You’ll also learn how to identify lawmakers trading stocks in industries where they might have inside information, violating the STOCK Act and padding the vast wealth gap between themselves and their constituents. Such stories are especially timely as momentum builds on Capitol Hill to ban members of Congress and their families from trading and owning stocks.

title: 60 years of frustration and fun: Happy birthday, FOIA!
speakers: David Cuillier; Jason Leopold; Lauren Harper
speaker_org_titles: Bloomberg | Brechner FOI Project, University of Florida | Ellsberg chair on government secrecy, Freedom of the Press Foundation
description_clean: This July marks the 60th anniversary of the signing of the U.S. Freedom of Information Act, and while not perfect, it has yielded amazing stories. This panel will run through the “best of” stories birthed because of FOIA, providing inspiration and story ideas for further requests.

title: Writing the investigative narrative
speaker_org_titles: Senior Enterprise Editor, NBC News Digital | Tampa Bay Times
description_clean: Learn how to make your investigative writing compelling and powerful. We cover everything from defining an investigative narrative to how to report, organize, write and self-edit your own! Topics include: Understanding the investigative narrative How to pitch Turning sources into characters Writing a scene Self-editing tips and techniques Organizing and writing a deeply reported investigative narrative Pre-registration is required and seating is limited.

title: SOLD OUT - Investigative skills: A master class
speaker_org_titles: The New York Times
description_clean: In this hands-on, tactical workshop using real case studies, we'll discuss investigative strategies to gather the information and sources needed to deliver a revelatory investigative piece -- in short, just about everything. How do you get started? How do you manage your progress to ensure your investigation is as targeted as possible? What tactics increase the likelihood of success? We'll talk about how to obtain the best publicly available databases to supercharge your reporting. We'll spend time on FOIA tactics, such as "threading" and "piggybacking," and how to stay organized when the records start rolling in. And we'll discuss the importance of building a chronology and other organizational tactics, including the use of AI tools such as Google Notebook and Gemini, that allow you to see into your own files and what other folks have already discovered. Pre-registration is required and seating is limited.

title: Leading past crisis: change, culture, and community
speaker_org_titles: Journalist and media consultant
description_clean: "Crisis” is a word we use constantly, probably too much, to describe the work we do. For many newsroom leaders, it has become an unfortunate and permanent state of being. Over the past year, colleagues have shared reflections like these: trying to fix what feels broken while enduring what’s out of their control; leading teams with fewer resources, more pressure, and less autonomy; working days that begin before sunrise and end long after dinner; uncertainty about whether the industry they know will still be there at the end of their careers; and a growing tension between loving the mission and purpose of journalism while questioning whether the work still loves them back. Too many wonder if the time has come to do something easier that pays more. Together, we’ll begin to tackle the visible challenges of leadership— how we can guide our team through change, managing our time and workload, delegating what we can and planning what we can’t; developing our team and holding them accountable for the work, and building a sustainable culture of excellence—while also making space for the less visible strains — anxiety, self-doubt, isolation, the emotional weight of responsibility and, laughably, having a life outside of work. And… this will not be hours of “woe is me.” If you’re still practicing the craft, serving your community, then you still believe in the strength and power of journalism. This deep dive will focus on optimism, honesty, and practical, tangible leadership skills—offering coaching, peer learning, and clear priorities for what to do next, how to care for yourself, and how to continue serving your community with purpose. Pre-registration is required and seating is limited.

title: 15 min break
Finding stories in IRS Filings
speaker_org_titles: Research Director and Investigative Journalist, Center for Media and Democracy
description_clean: In this training, journalists will learn how to find and read various IRS filings from 990s to 1023s, and how to identify potential stories within them. Attendees will be given concrete examples of stories that came through the analysis of IRS filings, and an opportunity to explore future story ideas. Bring questions! Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

title: Demo: The Gun Violence Data Hub
speaker_org_titles: Editor, Gun Violence Data Hub - The Trace | Managing Editor at The Trace
description_clean: Learn about the Gun Violence Data Hub, an initiative from The Trace, and how to use the data to report deeper and better stories in your own community. The Data Hub provides resources for newsrooms to bolster their U.S. gun violence knowledge, as well as an open data library of contextualized and well-documented datasets. This session is good for those with some experience using spreadsheets. Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

title: Behind the PR wall: An off-the-record chat
speaker_org_titles: Senior Investigative Platform Manager  | Senior National Investigative Reporter
description_clean: A candid conversation with leading current and former Public Information Officers from the federal government & private sector. They'll give the inside scoop on forging relationships, getting behind the PR wall and securing the responses & interviews you need for your stories **To allow our panelists to be candid, this session will be OFF THE RECORD - NO RECORDING/SOCIAL MEDIA POSTING** Submit your questions for our experts here: https://forms.gle/BuzK7oPfGszWAeTi7

title: Getting hostile sources to talk
speaker_org_titles: ESPN - Investigative Reporter
description_clean: How do you get someone to talk when they definitely don't want to talk to you? Perhaps they're the target of your investigation. Or perhaps they're afraid they'll lose their job, their credibility or maybe even their life. ESPN investigative reporter Tisha Thompson will moderate this special session with retired IRS Senior Special Agent Chris Seymour on how to get hostile sources to talk. He will share his tips and techniques on how to secure the interview, what to do when you know they're lying, and other challenging situations he's successfully navigated with all types of sources -- including celebrities, con artists and victims -- to secure hundreds of criminal convictions over the course of his three-decade career.

title: Best practices in AI-assisted data journalism
speakers: Jingnan Huo; Jonathan Soma; Robert Benincasa
speaker_org_titles: Columbia University | NPR | Senior Producer, Investigations, NPR
description_clean: From vibe coding to error message review and methodology design, we'll discuss some ideas and real-life examples on how data journalists can use AI tools effectively and ethically. Data journalists have navigated a series of technological innovations since the first NICAR conference in the early 1990s. How will we deal with the AI revolution? What’s working now and what’s likely to help us or bite us in the future? What are some guardrails and best practices? You'll learn how to use AI models to ingest, annotate and summarize large volumes of unstructured data, how to use AI to debug and generate code, and how to bring that work in line with ethical and accuracy standards. And we'll talk about some of the things that are best left to natural intelligence (humans).

title: Fields of evidence: Data-driven investigations into pesticide exposure
speakers: Ben Felder; Cynthia Barnett; Mark Horvit; Natasha Gilbert; Steve Sapienza
speaker_org_titles: Director of Climate and Environment Reporting Initiatives, University of Florida | Editor in Chief, Investigate Midwest | Freelance Investigative Journalist  | Senior Editor, Pulitzer Center | University of Missouri
description_clean: Pesticides are sprayed across millions of acres every year—but the impacts on nearby communities are often hidden in fragmented data, scattered regulations, and hard-to-reach sources. Reporters will share how they tracked pesticide use, mapped potential cancer and Parkinson's clusters, and connected environmental exposure to public health risks in farming communities. Panelists will discuss the datasets that make these investigations possible, strategies for finding agricultural sources in tight-lipped farming communities, and ways to turn complex environmental data into compelling visual and narrative storytelling.

title: OPEN MIC: News director forum, what keeps us up at night
speakers: Jodie Fleischer; Letitia Walker; Matt Goldberg; Matthew Mosk
speaker_org_titles: Cox Media Group, Managing Editor of Investigative Content and Collaborations | Head of CBS News Investigations | VP & News Director, CBS LA | WBRZ News Director
description_clean: A lively discussion (and support group) for Newsroom Managers to share your reality check on the evolution of television news. From navigating budget cuts and layoffs, to journalist safety, pivoting to new platforms, challenges finding qualified talent, what you're looking for in your next hire, and everything in between. Come join us to share your thoughts! This session will not be recorded.

title: Do try this at home: Tools and tips to investigate Washington from anywhere
speakers: Cheryl Phillips; Deborah J. Nelson; Derek Willis; Gabriel Pietrorazio
speaker_org_titles: Big Local News Founder/Lecturer, Stanford University | Senior Broadcast Field Correspondent, KJZZ | University of Maryland
description_clean: What happens in Washington doesn't stay in Washington. We'll provide a treasure trove of OSINT, digital tools, reporting strategies and IRE resources to help you (quickly and cheaply) follow the investigative trail from Washington to your community and back.

title: After the cut: Surviving and rebuilding investigative careers after buyouts and layoffs
speakers: Cindy Galli; Emma Carew Grovum; Martha Mendoza; Trenton Daniel
speaker_org_titles: Director, Justice and Legal Reporting, MS NOW | FRONTLINE/PBS | Healthbeat | Kimbap Media
description_clean: Day One: Don’t panic. Day Two: Sign out of your socials. Day Three: Make a To Do List. Buyouts and layoffs have become a recurring reality in investigative journalism, often disrupting long-term projects, professional identity, and financial stability. This session focuses on how investigative reporters can strategically manage the aftermath—professionally, practically, and psychologically—while continuing to do accountability work. Panelists will share concrete strategies for protecting investigations in progress, preserving sources and data, and navigating legal and ethical obligations during transitions. The session will explore career pathways beyond traditional newsrooms, including nonprofit investigative outlets, collaborations, fellowships, freelancing, teaching, and entrepreneurial models. Attendees will also share their experiences negotiating exits, understanding severance and noncompete clauses, rebuilding income streams, and maintaining investigative rigor outside legacy structures. Grounded in real experiences, this session aims to provide investigative journalists with tools not just to endure industry upheaval, but to adapt and continue producing impactful work.

title: Following the money: Crypto tracing for business and crime reporting
speakers: Annys Shin; Anthony Cormier; Spencer Woodman
speaker_org_titles: Bloomberg | ICIJ | The International Consortium of Investigative Journalists
description_clean: Criminals, sanctions-evaders and corrupt actors are increasingly turning to the alternate financial system of cryptocurrency to discreetly move money. A contradiction exists at the heart of crypto: The technology offers users deep anonymity, but it also records key details of most transactions in a public ledger known as the blockchain. With the right tools, analyzing these public records can lead to big reporting breaks. This session will walk participants through the specific methodologies behind ICIJ's year-long Coin Laundry investigation, which showed how the world's largest crypto firms have profited of off scams, hacks and other crimes. The session will be targeted toward a wide audience. Previous experience in crypto investigations will not be necessary. The session can be broken into three general sections. First: Crypto 101. We'll first walk participants through some 101 basics. What is cryptocurrency? What is a crypto currency address? What are blockchains and what are the meaningful differences for reporters between the blockchains? Second: How to gather basic crypto intel. Here, we'll show the crowd how ICIJ identified cryptocurrency addresses tied to individuals, crime groups or companies we were interested in covering. We will show participants where to look for illicit cryptocurrency addresses and how to verify the accuracy of crypto address ownership. Here, we will introduce participants to the excellent tool Arkham Intelligence. We’ll show participants how to use this tool and also discuss its limitations. Third, How to start tracing crypto transactions. Here we'll show participants how, once they have identified a cryptocurrency address of interest, to begin simple tracing using open-source tools. A key part of this is using primary source material to fact-check the tracing. As part of this process, it's important to walk participants through basic points on how mainstream crypto exchanges work. This will include a brief discussion on how to leverage APIs of open source blockchain explorers like Arkham Intelligence and Tronscan to get solid analysis. At this point in the presentation, I can invite participants to open their laptops and do a simple cryptocurrency trace using Arkham Intelligence, and then perform a simple fact-check of Arkham's tracing results. Participants can also bring cryptocurrency addresses they are interested in examining to the panel to look at in real-time. (I am pitching this as a panel but it could likely be done in workshop format as well.) Finally, we'll have a few closing comments on best practices we learned around having experts help validate and expand on our crypto tracing.

title: 15 min break
Investigate like the AP: Using Everlaw legal tools in news
speakers: Aaron Kessler; Aaron Patton; Chris Miles; Lena Inthisenesouk
speaker_org_titles: Media Partnerships Lead, Everlaw for Good | Sales Development Representative | Solutions Architect, Everlaw | The Associated Press, Investigative Data Journalist
description_clean: Aaron Kessler, data and investigative journalist at the Associated Press, talks about how his team uses Everlaw in document-heavy investigations. Also see how Everlaw's document review and discovery platform can help reporters dig deeper into large document sets, understand data, surface insights, and streamline their investigations to chart a straighter path to the truth. Learn more at: https://www.everlaw.com/forgood/

tracks: Beat reporting; Data analysis; Hands on; Story ideas
title: Basic campaign finance for the 2026 midterms
speaker_org_titles: Data Journalist, Howard Center for Investigative Journalism | Elections & Investigations Reporter, Central Florida Public Media
description_clean: With the midterms coming up this year, there are more opportunities than ever to investigate local, state and federal campaign finances. In this session, we'll practice navigating FEC filings, finding key figures and turning them into compelling stories. We'll also review common pitfalls in FEC data, such as how to avoid double-counting pass-through PAC contributions and how to classify in-kind contributions (and what those are!). Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

title: The art of the interview
speakers: Allya Davidson; David Hammer; Julie Watts
speaker_org_titles: CBS News California Investigative Correspondent  | Chief Investigative Reporter, WWL Louisiana | Executive producer, the fifth estate, Canadian Broadcasting Corporation
description_clean: From finding people to talk and convincing them to go on camera, to tough accountability interviews and execution. We'll share tips on getting the best emotion from your interviewee, unscheduled/accountability interviews. We'll also share ways to avoid re-traumatizing victims when addressing tough conversations.

title: AMA for high performers
speaker_org_titles: Chief Rebellion Officer for Rebellion Collective
description_clean: Feeling stuck, undervalued, or out of alignment in your career? You’re not alone...and you don’t have to figure it out on your own either. Join us for an open, no-BS conversation about what it takes to navigate change with clarity, courage, and self-respect. Whether you’re negotiating a raise, plotting an exit, or redefining what success means to you, this session is a space to ask questions, get grounded, and walk away with real tools for reclaiming your career on your terms.

title: Networking: Students
speaker_org_titles: News Editor, The Cougar (University of Houston)
description_clean: Mix and mingle with peers from throughout the world to find solidarity, make new friends, and build your professional community in this fun and informal networking session. This session is for students.

title: Investigating the impacts of the oil and gas industry
speakers: Justin Nobel; Martha Pskowski; Nick Bowlin
speaker_org_titles: Investigative science journalist, Rolling Stone, DeSmog | Reporter with The Frontier and ProPublica  | Texas reporter, Inside Climate News
description_clean: Learn from reporters covering the impacts of the oil and gas industry in local communities around the country. Fracking technology has allowed oil and gas production in the United States to reach record highs. But as new environmental problems emerge, the oil and gas industry is also contending with the side effects of aging, dilapidated infrastructure. In this session we will share reporting tips from reporters working in Oklahoma, Texas, Ohio and Pennsylvania to humanize the oil and gas story. They will share how to substantiate claims of environmental and health harms, where to find data sets chock-full of stories, and how to track oil and gas waste streams. We will discuss the impacts of deregulation under the Trump administration and how oil and gas accountability reporting can reach audiences across the political divide. We will also discuss how the Iran war is impacting the U.S. oil and gas industry.

title: Protecting yourself and sources in your raw tape
speakers: Chiara Eisner; Rob Antle; Ximena Bustillo
speaker_org_titles: CBC News, senior producer, Atlantic investigative unit | DHS + Immigration Correspondent | NPR, investigative correspondent
description_clean: When you're collecting hours and hours of tape with sources who need varied levels of protection, having a good organizational system matters more than ever. We talk about why you might not want to keep your tape forever, how to think about using transcription and other services on the cloud, and other issues that come up with sound and video recording.

title: Don't outsource your instincts: Traditional research skills matter more in the AI era
speaker_org_titles: Newmark J-School, Investigative Research Instructor and Associate Professor | Research Editor
description_clean: The latest tips to upgrade your traditional search skills to responsibly and ethically incorporate AI tools into your investigative research and news-gathering process. Deepen your search skills, don't outsource and lose them!

title: Information session: Today's News for Tomorrow
speaker_org_titles: Director of craft and local news, Poynter Institute  | Investigative Reporters and Editors
description_clean: In this session, speakers will answer questions about Today's News for Tomorrow, a collaboration between the Internet Archive, Investigative Reporters and Editors and the Poynter Institute, with support from Press Forward. The program offers newsrooms free training on digital preservation and sustained support to ensure the long-term preservation and access of their digital assets.

title: How to find immigration stories in your community
speakers: Aarón Torres; Lautaro Grinspan; Nicole Foy; Wendy Fry
speaker_org_titles: CalMatters immigration reporter | Immigration Reporter at The Atlanta Journal-Constitution | politics and immigration reporter | ProPublica investigative reporter
description_clean: Immigration reporting goes so far beyond coverage of the border and immigration court, but too often tips focus on national scope. Many of the biggest immigration stories during the last year started locally, and local reporters who know the basics of how immigration systems work in the communities they cover are poised to tell the best version of these stories. This panel will cover the basics that local reporters who want to tell immigration stories in their community should know – establishing agency contacts, understanding the detention and deportation pathway in your area, leveraging hyperlocal networks tracking immigration arrests, how to comb for location-specific data from detentions to courts and how to build sources to tell immigration stories that touch arts, science, health, sports and more.

title: Lunch
Coaching AI tools for investigative reporting
speaker_org_titles: Senior Editor of Data Journalism at The Atlanta Journal-Constitution
description_clean: AI tools like ChatGPT and Claude are most useful when you coach them, giving clear direction, pushing back on bad answers, and iterating until you get what you need. In this hands-on session, we'll use AI to draft targeted FOIA requests and iterate through them; make sense of an unfamiliar dataset by generating a data dictionary and identifying story questions; get help with Excel and spreadsheet tasks without writing code; and debug scripts when something breaks. You'll leave with reusable prompt patterns and a clear sense of where AI helps your reporting and where it will confidently make things up. No coding experience required. Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training and have a free ChatGPT or Claude account.

title: Google Sheets: Importing and data prep
description_clean: Don't give up if your data isn't presented in a neat spreadsheet. This session will teach you how to get data into a spreadsheet and prepare it for analysis. We will look at how to import text files, deal with data in a PDF, and get a table on a web page into a spreadsheet. This session is good for: Anyone comfortable working in Google Sheets. You must bring your own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class, and you will need a free Google account to participate.

title: The 10 things I've learned
speakers: Chris Chmura; Cristin Severance; Kyle Iboshi
speaker_org_titles: Consumer Reporter, KNTV-TV (NBC Bay Area) | Investigative Reporter, KGW-TV (Portland, Oregon) | WRAL Investigative Reporter and Documentary Producer
description_clean: One of IRE’s most popular sessions each year, this panel brings together veteran journalists to share the lessons that shaped their careers, changed their reporting, and helped them survive the chaos of this business. From investigations and storytelling to mistakes, burnout, leadership, and life outside the newsroom, this session is packed with practical advice, honest moments, and a little humor. Expect real talk, memorable stories, and takeaways you can actually use, no matter where you are in your career.

title: Networking: LGBTQ
speaker_org_titles: Investigative Reporters & Editors | Investigative Reporters & Editors, Training Director and Managing Editor
description_clean: What better way to savor Pride month than to be among your community. Join us to mix, mingle and build community. This session is for people who identify as part of the LGBTQ+ community.

title: Inside the housing crisis: What every reporter needs to know
speaker_org_titles: Communications manager for Eviction Lab
description_clean: The housing crisis touches nearly every community — from record housing costs to rising homelessness to millions of evictions each year — yet reporting on it can feel daunting. Where are the real stories? How do you navigate the technical data? And how can newsroom work lead to accountability or change? In this session, three experts from data, academia, and storytelling break down the biggest trends, the overlooked angles, and the most useful tools for reporters on any beat. Walk away with story ideas, data sources, and a clearer roadmap for covering one of the most urgent issues in the country. Moderated by Princeton’s Eviction Lab.

title: Filing better FOIAs (getting to documents via people, and vice versa)
speakers: Josh Eidelson; Kate Martin; Suzy Khimm
speaker_org_titles: APM reports | National Reporter for NBC News | Senior reporter for Bloomberg Businessweek
description_clean: This session will explore how to increase your chances of receiving records that reveal significant new information to advance your story. Who and what can help you identify what records you should ask for in the first place? And how can you make itmore likely that you’ll actually receive a useful response? We will look at strategies for formulating and pursuing public records requests — treating them as an distinct reporting task, often requiring cultivating relationships, conducting interviews and other digging to help pinpoint what records could be most helpful to ask for, and ease the path to actually getting them. We will also look at how you can make the most of records to help identify potential sources for further reporting.

title: Video show & tell: Breaking news investigations on deadline with Jatara McGee
speaker_org_titles: WPXI-TV
description_clean: Sign up to “Show & Tell” us about your best work and get constructive feedback from the moderator and audience. Sign-ups are required for presenters. Each presenter will have 15 minutes to share their work and receive feedback. All are welcome to attend! Sign up here: https://www.signupgenius.com/go/10C084CA8AC29A4FBCE9-64417344-video *These sessions will not be recorded.

title: Reporting from exile: How to cover authoritarian regimes, protect sources and build trust
speakers: Clavel Rangel; Fatemeh Jamalpour; Tenzin Pema; Tibisay Zea
speaker_org_titles: Freelancer | Independent Journalist, Knight Wallace Fellow 26' | Journalist - Tibet, Knight-Wallace Fellow 2025-2026 | Latin America correspondent for The World radio program
description_clean: Journalists covering authoritarian regimes often continue their work from exile—but repression does not stop at the border. This panel explores how reporters investigate power across borders while navigating surveillance, threats to family members, and fear within diaspora communities. Drawing on experiences from Iran, Cuba, Venezuela, and Tibet, panelists will share practical strategies to: protect sources and collaborators across borders; build trust with communities living under fear and trauma; navigate digital and physical security risks in exile and sustain investigative work from outside the country.

title: Media law for journalists: Newsgathering
speaker_org_titles: Deputy Director, Media Law Resource Center
description_clean: In this session, you’ll learn from an experienced media attorney about the laws governing newsgathering activity, including going undercover, entering private property, using technology, gaining access to government proceedings and records, dealing with confidential sources, and more. We’ll also talk specifically about coverage of immigration enforcement and protests.

title: The new tactics and technologies of war
So you wanna make a podcast?
speakers: Jason Moon; Kara McGuirk-Allison; Rhaina Cohen
speaker_org_titles: Center for investigative Reporting/Reveal/More To The Story | Producer/Editor, Embedded | Senior Reporter/Producer, New Hampshire Public Radio
description_clean: This workshop is designed for the audio-curious who feel like they don't know where to start. What staffing do you need? How much might it cost? How long might it take? How can you make something that people will actually hear? How do you approach storyboarding, getting comfortable with the gear, scripting, voicing/tracking, storytelling? We'll cover enough of the basics that attendees will feel equipped to pitch and begin developing their ideas. Pre-registration is required and seating is limited.

title: Searching in the age of AI
speaker_org_titles: Indicator

title: 15 min break
Find public records and leaks: The new OCCRP Aleph Pro
speaker_org_titles: Product Manager for Data Platforms, Organized Crime and Corruption Reporting Project
description_clean: You’ve got a leak, a name, or a suspicious company — what’s your next move? In a world of complex financial secrecy, uncovering the truth demands data power. Join this hands-on workshop to master the all-new OCCRP Aleph Pro, holding over four billion records of leaks, sanctions, corporate registries, and beyond. For years, Aleph has been the backbone of hundreds of investigations, and we have now evolved it to meet the challenges of modern reporting. Bring your laptop and learn to cross-reference targets, generate structured entities from raw text, and map hidden connections into visual networks. You will also learn how to secure access to protected leaks curated for journalists, activists, and researchers. It's time to power your next investigation using Aleph Pro. Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

title: Catching the quiet changes: Automating website monitoring with Visualping
speaker_org_titles: Visualping
description_clean: In this session, you'll learn how to use Visualping, an AI website change detection tool, to automatically monitor and get instant alerts for important changes to any websites you need to track for your beat, whether it's local court dockets, government agency websites, a politician's social media pages, or something else. We'll walk through setting up a Visualping alert, using different features and settings, and tips for using Visualping's AI criteria and summary features. Attendees are welcome to sign up for a free Visualping account at visualping.io to follow along. Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

title: Scroll-stopping investigations: What works in vertical video
speaker_org_titles: Professor, Northeastern University/Investigative Reporter, WCVB-TV
description_clean: Vertical video can help investigative journalism reach audiences who may never watch a newscast or read a long-form story — but not every story works the same way on TikTok, Instagram Reels, YouTube Shorts or other social platforms. In this session, journalists who are successfully producing vertical video will break down what works, what doesn’t, and how to make strong editorial choices without sacrificing depth, accuracy or impact. From format and scripting to audience strategy and monetization, this panel will offer practical takeaways for reporters and newsrooms at every stage.

title: Gossip, group chats and encrypted drives: How to root out non-public records and keep sources safe
speakers: Elizabeth Koh; John Skinner; Sara DiNatale; Susie Neilson
speaker_org_titles: Freedom of the Press Foundation -- SecureDrop Engineering Manager | Investigative Reporter, The Boston Globe | Investigative Reporter, the San Francisco Chronicle | the San Francisco Chronicle, Policy and politics reporter
description_clean: When journalists talk about obtaining records, often it's in the context of using open records laws to pry documents from reticent government officials. But what about when the documents you need aren't held by the government — they're tucked away in a hospital's record system, or on a scammy salesperson's iPhone? What if you don't even know what records you're looking for? During this panel, reporters Susie Neilson, Sara DiNatale and Elizabeth Koh will talk about creative techniques they've used for sourcing sensitive, non-public records, and how they used these records to produce impactful and award-winning investigations. Finally, John Skinner of SecureDrop will offer tips on how to keep sensitive sources safe from retribution by vindictive bosses or companies.

title: Records access and the right to record: Newsgathering for covering law enforcement
description_clean: Video of law enforcement in action and related public records are a powerful tool in accountability journalism — from covering police use-of-force incidents and protest responses, to immigration raids and other enforcement operations that happen out of public view. But the legal landscape around capturing and accessing footage of law enforcement, and obtaining public records around the actions of individual officers and departments, is complex and changing. This session brings together journalists and legal experts to explore shifts in the right to record officers in public, including ongoing legal challenges to police “buffer zone” laws nationwide; using local law enforcement footage to scrutinize federal immigration enforcement; the use of drones for newsgathering amid efforts to establish no-fly zones around law enforcement operations; and the varied sources for public records and footage that can be mined to investigate problem offers and departments.

title: Bringing collaborative investigations to life across print and audio
speakers: Brian Howey; Mukta Joshi; Najib Aminy; Nate Rosenfield; Sona Patel
speaker_org_titles: Local Investigations Fellow, The New York Times | Program and Editorial Director, Local Investigations Fellowship, The New York Times | Reporter and producer, Reveal  | The Bell Project | The Bell Project - Co-Founder
description_clean: Collaborations between print and audio journalists have the power to bring stories to life, take listeners inside the reporting process and produce the kind of deeply engaging work that defines modern journalism. In this panel, you'll hear from journalists who successfully navigated print-audio collaborations that have resonated nationally. They'll also explore how to adapt an investigation for different platforms and how to juggle managing podcast production, reporting and editing needs for print stories. They’ll also discuss the pros and cons of working in two mediums simultaneously.

title: Video show & tell: Anything goes with Kara Kenney
speaker_org_titles: WRTV Investigative Reporter
description_clean: Sign up to “Show & Tell” us about your best work and get constructive feedback from the moderator and audience. Sign-ups are required for presenters. Each presenter will have 15 minutes to share their work and receive feedback. All are welcome to attend! Sign up here: https://www.signupgenius.com/go/10C084CA8AC29A4FBCE9-64418022-video *These sessions will not be recorded.

title: The health care beat: following the money, power, and patients
speakers: Cynthia Dizikes; Daniel Chang; Joaquin Palomino; Katie Campbell
speaker_org_titles: Investigative Reporter, San Francisco Chronicle | KFF Health News | ProPublica documentary filmmaker / journalist | San Francisco Chronicle Investigative Reporter
description_clean: Reporting on health care can feel confusing and overwhelming. The good news is that there are reams of public records and data to get you started at both the state and federal level. This session will show you where to begin when launching on a health care investigation, from official accounts of deficient care to detailed financial records that can shed light on business decisions that impact staff and patients. We'll also explore the challenges and careful considerations that must be made when filming inside hospitals and detailing patient stories.

title: Save Our Signs: Reporting on the federal push to rewrite history
speaker_org_titles: Associate Director, Mapping Prejudice | Social Sciences Librarian
description_clean: In March 2025, the Trump administration issued Executive Order 14253, entitled “Restoring Truth and Sanity to American History,” which ordered a massive review of public monuments, signs and exhibits at National Park Service (NPS) sites to ensure that they do not “inappropriately disparage Americans past or living.” At least 57 educational signs have already been removed or altered at 10 NPS sites, and, according to a leaked internal database, government officials have flagged educational materials for review at more than 150 NPS sites. In response, a group of librarians and public historians formed Save Our Signs, a crowdsourced research project to create the Save Our Signs Archive: a centralized, open access archive dedicated to photos of National Parks signs. The SOS Archive has already been used by many journalists. In this panel, Save Our Signs team members will outline the real scope and impacts of EO 14253 and debunk common misconceptions. Attendees will learn about the opportunities and challenges facing journalists working with government sources, ranging from data sets, documents, media, and even interpretative materials at National Parks sites. In addition, we will discuss the opportunities and challenges of working with crowdsourced collections like the SOS Archive.

title: Teaching the truth: innovations and imperatives in investigative journalism education for 2026
speakers: Liz Lucas; Martha Mendoza; Mc Nelly Torres; Michael Corey
speaker_org_titles: Associate Director, Mapping Prejudice | FRONTLINE/PBS | Independent Journalist, editor & forthcoming book author | Missouri School of Journalism
description_clean: The landscape of misinformation, media disruption, and AI is evolving quickly, and so must we educators adjust to prepare the next generation of investigative journalists. This session presents contemporary approaches, pedagogical frameworks, and practical tools for teaching investigative reporting to undergraduates and graduate students in 2026. Participants will discuss how to integrate data literacy, digital verification, accountability reporting, collaborative methodologies, and ethical decision-making into our lessons, balanced with traditional skills like sourcing, narrative construction, and public records mastery. We’ll examine case studies of successful course designs, experiential learning models and strategies to help students navigate live investigations responsibly and safely. The session also addresses emerging challenges such as AI’s impact on reporting workflows and media credibility; teaching students to critically assess generated content; and preparing them for cross-border, multimedia, and solutions-oriented investigative work. Attendees will leave with actionable ideas for syllabi, assignments, and assessment practices that reflect the demands and possibilities of investigative journalism in 2026.

title: 15 min break
Turn document dumps into promising leads with Claude
speaker_org_titles: Data Journalist, Howard Center for Investigative Journalism
description_clean: Have you ever had to sort through hundreds—or even thousands—of public records to find the stories buried inside? In this hands-on session, you'll learn how to use the Claude API to sort through huge troves of documents and pull out the information you're looking for. We'll identify leads hidden in Foreign Agent Registration Act filings as a case study, and you'll walk away with a plug-and-play template that's ready to use for your own projects. Some coding experience is helpful for this session, but not required. Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

title: Introducing the new IRE Resource Center
speaker_org_titles: University of Maryland
description_clean: The new IRE Resource Center is here! Members can now access an enhanced, user-friendly search interface with more opportunities to easily surface thousands of tipsheets, contest entries and other training materials. In this session, the people behind the app will demonstrate its features, talk about how it was made and solicit live feedback from the audience.

title: Is it AI? Verifying video and other materials during breaking news
speaker_org_titles: Consumer Investigator, KUSA-TV, Denver | The LA Local, Reporter
description_clean: Description: When news breaks, one of the first things we do is head to social media to see if anyone nearby captured the moment. But how do you know if what you're seeing is real and not AI-generated? This panel will offer tips, tools and verification protocols for social media and other videos circulating on the internet.

title: Networking: Journalists of color, sponsored by ProPublica
speaker_org_titles: Investigative Reporter - FOX13 Memphis (WHBQ)
description_clean: Mix and mingle, meet friends old and new, and build your professional community in this fun and informal networking session. This session is for journalists of color. This session is sponsored by ProPublica. IRE retains control of content, including the topic and speaker selection, for all conference sessions.

title: Reporting on the U.S. military: Beyond the headlines
speakers: Dan Lamothe; Greg Jaffe; Leah Rosenbaum; Sonner Kehrt
speaker_org_titles: Investigative Reporter, The War Horse | The New York Times | The Washington Post
description_clean: Military reporting becomes front-page news when there's a war going on. But during peace time, it's often a neglected beat -- despite US taxpayers paying $850 billion to fund the military and another $325 billion on the VA health system. The military touches all of our lives, but remains opaque to the average American. This panel session led by reporters who cover the military as their primary beat will discuss how to find behind-the-scenes stories about the US military that can influence real change, both on a political and personal level.

title: Building the team: Where everyone wants to work and no one wants to leave
speaker_org_titles: Journalist and media consultant
description_clean: Leading and managing a broadcast newsroom has never been harder or more important. This session digs into the core skills today’s news leaders need, offering a pragmatic, honest, and shared conversation about what it takes to build a positive, inclusive culture where people want to do their best work and stay. We’ll focus on practical systems for onboarding, developing teams, re-energizing veteran staff, setting clear expectations for behavior, embedding feedback into daily work, and understanding what’s really happening inside your newsroom — all while centering community-based journalism as a driver of healthy internal culture. You’ll leave inspired, better equipped to handle tough challenges, and prepared to intentionally shape the culture that supports both your people and your strategy.

tracks: Beat reporting; Criminal legal system; Equity, inclusion and accessibility; Reporting & writing strategies; Story ideas
title: Working with victims in a trauma-informed way
speaker_org_titles: UC Berkeley Journalism School

title: Diseases and deadlines: Covering health issues on deadline and beyond
speakers: Catherine Sweeney; Eli Cahan; Madi Alexander
speaker_org_titles: Deputy Data & Graphics editor, POLITICO | Health reporter, Nashville Public Radio | Journalist / Physician, Rolling Stone / Stanford University
description_clean: Measles, mumps, rubella, oh my! Health care is driving major political and public interest debates, from measles outbreaks to broader policy fights about affordability and cost. This panel will help reporters identify meaningful health stories and turn breaking health news into deeper enterprise and investigative reporting. Using several timely health topics, reporters from digital, magazine, and local radio outlets will discuss how they would approach the same issues on three timelines: 2 days, 2 weeks, and 2 months. Attendees will learn how to identify breaking health stories with have real investigative potential and move from fast-turn news coverage to deeper enterprise reporting.

title: The evolution of anonymity in the Trump era
description_clean: In the current climate, newsrooms are being forced to reconsider what qualifies for anonymous treatment in a story. We talk about the evolving risks our sources face and how to systemize the decision of whether to remove someone's name from an already-published story.

title: Recharge Room
description_clean: Need a spot to decompress, take a load off, get your bearings or recharge your batteries? This is the room for you!

title: Game Night
description_clean: Have some fun with friends old and new while playing board games, cards, etc. Games and cards are provided, and feel free to bring your own. All conference attendees are welcome.

title: Registration Hours
description_clean: Pick up your badge or register on-site: 7:30 a.m. - 5:30 p.m.

title: Mentor Breakfast (invite only)
description_clean: If you signed up for the conference mentor program, come meet your match at this invitation-only breakfast.

title: Exhibitors
description_clean: Stop by and visit with the exhibitors and recruiters.

title: Adjacent Event: Breakfast Chat About ProPublica's Local Reporting Network
description_clean: Are you a local reporter or editor with an ambitious investigative project in mind? Please join us to learn more about the Local Reporting Network, a collaborative program through which ProPublica works with local newsrooms on a year-long accountability project. This opportunity is open to both staff and freelance reporters working with a local/regional newsroom on the project. Please join us for an informal breakfast chat about the program and how it works. You'll meet both ProPublica editors and reporters who can talk about their participation in the program.

title: Audio Coaching 1:1
description_clean: Sign up for 30 minutes with an audio pro to use however you like: Bring tape for a critique, a pitch for workshopping or your questions about surviving and thriving in the audio journalism space.

title: Barry Nash and Company One-on-One Coaching
description_clean: "Barry Nash and his team return to IRE to provide personalized coaching sessions. If you have questions about any aspect of your delivery — your appearance, how you use your voice, how you can perform most effectively in the field or in the studio — this is an opportunity you won’t want to miss. Barry has been working with talent at all levels of experience and ability for over 30 years. He currently authors the only blog in the world focused exclusively on television news and sports performance. You can find it on Substack Please note: Participants should bring recent footage on a flash drive, webpage/online link, or storage drive (Google or Dropbox). Sign up for a time slot here: https://www.signupgenius.com/go/10C0445A9AD28A5FEC34-64383836-irecoaching#/"

title: The business of freelancing workshop
speakers: Info at The IIJ; Katherine Reynolds Lewis; Shernay Williams
speaker_org_titles: Info at The IIJ
description_clean: Presented by the Institute for Independent Journalists. Join other freelance journalists for a full-day workshop that will teach you the path to financially and emotionally sustainable freelancing! You'll receive 6 live, interactive hours of class time and a package of worksheets, freelance tools, and resources to rely on for years to come. Taught personally by IIJ leaders Katherine Reynolds Lewis and Shernay Williams. Hear directly from editors at National Geographic, Mother Jones and others. Pre-registration is required and seating is limited.

tracks: Reporting & writing strategies; Research; Story ideas
title: Finding forever chemicals in your community: A hands-on guide to PFAS data
speaker_org_titles: The Atlanta Journal-Constitution
description_clean: PFAS "forever chemicals" have contaminated drinking water systems across the United States, and the data to start investigating is already public. This hands-on session will show reporters how to find contamination and potential industrial sources in the communities they cover using freely available federal datasets. We'll walk through EPA's Unregulated Contaminant Monitoring Rule (UCMR) data to identify which public water systems have detected PFAS and at what levels, then use EPA's ECHO database to pinpoint nearby facilities that manufacture or discharge these chemicals. Attendees will leave with a step-by-step workflow they can apply to their own coverage area as soon as they get home. No prior data experience required. Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

title: Consumer confidential
speaker_org_titles: Consumer Investigator  | WSB-TV
description_clean: Great consumer investigations start with stories people actually care about — and reporters who know how to make those stories stand out. In this session, journalists and consumer reporters will share real-world tips on finding compelling content, building reliable sources, and spotting the everyday issues that impact viewers most.
