The panel will also dive into how reporters can separate themselves in a crowded media landscape by developing a strong brand, connecting with audiences, and telling stories in ways that get noticed across platforms. Whether you’re new to consumer reporting or looking to sharpen your investigative edge, this conversation will offer practical ideas you can take back to your newsroom right away.

title: Audio storytelling across languages and cultures
description_clean: TBD

title: Covering extreme right influences on your beat
speakers: Dana Coester; David Armiak; Evan Vorpahl; Kyle Spencer
speaker_org_titles: Director of State Courts and Democracy, True North Research | Reporting Right/The Pro-Democracy Info Lab | Research Director and Investigative Journalist, Center for Media and Democracy | WVU Media Innovation Center, Rural Digital Resilience Project, 100 Days in Appalachia,
description_clean: The Far Right is not just a collection of loud personalities in the Trump administration. It is a complex network of disparate groups that often work together. Ultraconservative funders, Christian Right advocacy organizations, white supremacists, social media influencers, and local legislators who have extreme goals often work together to wield enormous power in the communities you cover. This panel will offer a useful guide for reporters who want to take local stories (about extremism) and use them to shed light on how this larger national movement works to push public policy and specific agendas. We’ll share tools for finding hidden funding streams, tracking dark-money organizations, exposing behind-the-scenes political influencers, and reporting on astro-turf pipelines. We’ll talk about how to pitch these stories to editors, how to cover them in ways that help local audiences better understand the national forces at work, and how to stay safe while reporting on them.

title: Mining the mountain: Turning massive document dumps into breakthrough stories
speaker_org_titles: Investigative Reporter, News 4 and Fox San Antonio | Investigative Reporter/Anchor, Fox San Antonio
description_clean: From the Robb Elementary investigative records to the Epstein files, journalists are increasingly faced with sprawling, unstructured document dumps that can overwhelm even experienced reporters. This training session breaks down how to turn thousands of pages into focused, high-impact stories for broadcast, digital, and ongoing investigative coverage. Participants will learn how to prepare for these document dumps, strategies for getting through thousands of pages, organizational systems, finding the stories in the stack, and tracking follow-ups.

title: Video show & tell: Photogs and editors: Show your best visuals
speaker_org_titles: Investigative Reporter, KXAN-TV | Production Manager, InvestigateTV+/Gray Media
description_clean: Sign up to “Show & Tell” us about your best work and get constructive feedback from the moderator and audience. Sign-ups are required for presenters. Each presenter will have 15 minutes to share their work and receive feedback. All are welcome to attend! Sign up here: https://www.signupgenius.com/go/10C084CA8AC29A4FBCE9-64417265-video *These sessions will not be recorded.

title: Working it out: How editors & reporters work together, struggle together & produce great stories
speakers: Alyssa Katz; MIchael Hudson; Mike Rezendes
speaker_org_titles: Editor-in-Chief, The Forward | Head of US Investigations, the Guardian | Investigative reporter; author
description_clean: Relationships between reporters and editors can be fraught. They can be uplifting. They can be a bit of both. This session looks at the pitfalls, puzzlements and triumphs of working together and, sometimes, working at odds with each other. What do reporters need from editors? What do editors need from reporters? Panelists include journalists who have worked on both sides of the equation, including multiple Pulitzer Prize and IRE award winners.

title: Investigative journalism amid a decline in democracy
speakers: Drew Sullivan; Emilia Díaz-Struck; Ewald Scharfenberg; Kathy Jones
speaker_org_titles: Armando.info, Co-Editor | Co-Founder and Publisher, OCCRP | Committee to Protect Journalists | Global Investigative Journalism Network
description_clean: With more investigative journalists working in countries with authoritarian regimes or a decline in democracy, independent reporters have developed methods and techniques to continue holding power to account, while reducing risks connected to the context in which the reporting is taking place. While freedom of the press continues to deteriorate, strategies applied by journalists as well as collaborations, have been key to continue uncovering stories of public interest.

title: You just got hit by a natural disaster. Here's what to investigate.
speaker_org_titles: Chief Investigative Reporter/Evening Anchor WSMV-TV | Investigative Reporter KXAN News
description_clean: Natural disasters happen in every market. Regardless if you're facing tornadoes, wildfires, floods, global warming or ice storms, your viewers and readers will rely on you to investigate the systems in place that respond to the crises. Three journalists who have investigated system failures following natural disasters will show you what to immediately investigate and make your coverage stand out from your competition.

title: How explaining your sources builds trust
speaker_org_titles: Assistant Director, Trusting News
description_clean: Investigative journalism often asks audiences to trust reporting that took months or years to produce and that may include serious allegations against powerful people. In an era of intense scrutiny and accusations of bias, simply publishing the findings isn’t always enough. Increasingly, audiences want to understand how journalists reached their conclusions. This session explores how investigative reporters can build trust by being more transparent about their sourcing and reporting decisions. Drawing on research from Trusting News and examples from investigative outlets, the session will share practical ways journalists can explain their sourcing without compromising safety or confidentiality. We’ll discuss strategies such as explaining why certain sources were chosen, how sources were vetted, who was contacted but declined to comment, and what steps reporters took to verify claims. We’ll also look at how journalists can proactively address common audience questions about bias by showing their reporting process. Attendees will leave with concrete language, examples, and habits they can incorporate into investigative stories, helping audiences better understand the rigor behind their work and strengthening trust in high-stakes reporting. Pre-registration is required and seating is limited.

title: Reporting and writing for scene
description_clean: When it comes to crafting scenes, we can learn a lot from people who write fiction and screenplays. But our challenges begin long before the writing. Our scenes have to be true not just emotionally, but factually. This class will look at the importance of dialogue (dialogue >>> quotes); ways to hook and pull (and weave); and how to write a scene that readers will remember (all, without sacrificing the element of accountability — because, that’s what we do). We’ll dissect song lyrics; chat about creating dread; read a scene on the page, then watch it dramatized on screen. Plus, the incomparable Lizzie Presser (her first IRE!) will drop in and answer questions about some of her most unforgettable work. Pre-registration is required and seating is limited.

title: Claude Code for journalists: A (responsible) hands-on introduction to AI-powered data exploration
speaker_org_titles: Howard Center for Investigative Journalism
description_clean: Journalists are increasingly expected to work with data and documents, but many lack programming backgrounds. Claude Code — an AI coding tool that works on the desktop, command line or IDE — can help bridge that gap, letting reporters do some too-complicated-for-Excel analytical work without writing code from scratch. This three-hour, pre-registered hands-on class will give attendees a practical, start-to-finish introduction to Claude Code in a journalism context. Attendees will learn how to use Claude Code to do initial exploration of unfamiliar datasets, spot data quality issues that could undermine later findings, build simple web apps for exploring and visualizing data, develop a data analysis plan for a reporting project, and work through document sets efficiently. Just as importantly, attendees will learn what Claude Code can't do well, where it's likely to lead you astray, and how to verify its output — because responsible use matters as much as knowing the tool. No prior programming experience required. Attendees will leave with a repeatable workflow they can bring back to their newsrooms immediately. Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

title: 15 min break

tracks: Beat reporting; Data analysis; Tools & tech
title: Finding the story: Trade data
speaker_org_titles: Data and Graphics Reporter, POLITICO
description_clean: As trade and tariffs become an increasingly relevant issue, this session will demo a free (but very unintuitive) tool from the U.S. Census: USA Trade! USA Trade has comprehensive, district-level data (with lots of hidden gems) that can be useful for data journalists across beats, especially those relating to global economics, trade, tech, and foreign policy. This session will demonstrate how to use USA Trade and discuss some ways to understand and use the data in your reporting. This session is good for everyone interested in learning more about trade statistics. No data experience is necessary! Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

title: AI that actually knows what's happening: Live web research for working journalists
description_clean: AI to help you research a story, it draws on a snapshot of the internet from a single point in time. It doesn't know what was published today, and it will answer confidently anyway. This session shows you the fix. We'll walk through prompt engineering for real journalism research and how to structure requests that produce sourced, credible, organized information rather than confident-sounding summaries of old data. Then we'll connect it to the live web and run the same prompt again. The difference is immediate and visible. No coding required. Free tools provided. You'll walk out with something you can use on your next story. *Must have an account (can be free tier) with GPT.

title: Your investigation reveals a problem. Adding a solutions story shows it doesn't have to be this way.
speakers: George Chidi; Peter Gill; Tina Rosenberg
speaker_org_titles: co-founder, Solutions Journalism Network | Newsday transportation reporter
description_clean: Would a solutions angle help my story? How do I find one? This panel will feature two investigo-solutions reporters, and how and why they chose to cover solutions. Attendees will also learn a simple three-step process for finding the right solutions angle for their investigation.

tracks: Beat reporting; Business; Deep dive; Reporting & writing strategies; Story ideas
title: Art of the business investigation
speakers: Alex Campbell; Kendall Taggart; Scott Zamost
speaker_org_titles: Business Investigations Editor, The New York Times | CNBC Senior Investigative Producer | Investigative Reporter, Bloomberg News
description_clean: Hear from top business journalists in print, digital and broadcast dissect the unique challenges in navigating corporate America from how to find a variety of company records to getting reluctant subjects to talk. We'll show examples of how to turn a "no" into a "yes" from PR and crisis management firms hired to protect a company's image. We'll also detail how to get current and former employees to open up, and how to obtain and analyze obscure documents.

title: Should I even keep doing this?
speaker_org_titles: 10TampaBayNews | CBS News Investigative Unit
description_clean: In the face of never-ending change, we're forced to ask the question - is it all worth it? We talk about home life, marriage and kids, moving from city to city, constant upheaval in the business and mistrust of journalists. It's stressful! How do you successfully navigate the business and a personal life and if it's all changing, do you keep doing it all? Two veteran journalists, moms and friends start the conversation - you finish it!

title: From classroom to newsroom: Finding your footing after school
speakers: Adriana Navarro; Aidan Hughes; April Quevedo; Katherine Dailey
speaker_org_titles: Data Journalist, Howard Center for Investigative Journalism | Investigative and Data Journalist, Arizona Center for Investigative Reporting | Reporter, Michigan Advance | Reporter, Neighborhood Dispatch at Milwaukee Journal Sentinel
description_clean: Transitioning from internships and student journalism to your first real newsroom can be an exciting, and daunting, leap. In this session, four early-career journalists share the steps they took to jump-start their careers after graduation—and how you can do the same. From landing your first job to establishing yourself in a new city or beat, this session is great for recent journalism grads, current students, and those looking to pivot into journalism from other careers.

title: Media law in the second Trump Administration
speaker_org_titles: Deputy Director, Media Law Resource Center
description_clean: The second presidency of Donald Trump has upended norms and legal understandings regarding the ability of journalists to do their jobs without retaliation from federal and state governments. We’ll discuss how reporters and news organizations are navigating – or failing to navigate – these threats and challenges while exercising their First Amendment rights.

title: Pitching national shows and podcasts
speaker_org_titles: Executive Producer, Enterprise Storytelling Unit at NPR | Reveal from The Center for Investigative Reporting
description_clean: TBD

title: 15 min break
International networking
speaker_org_titles: Independent Journalist, South Korea
description_clean: Let’s talk about international journalism networks. This session is an informal chat for international journalists, first-time attendees, and anyone interested in global journalism opportunities to meet, exchange ideas, and share practical tips. We will discuss how journalists can build connections across countries, host regional meetups, and find fellowship opportunities around the world. I will also share lessons learned from organizing the first Seoul meetup and ideas for Islamabad meetup discussion. Also, you can offer your idea or plan on international meetup. First-time attendees are especially welcome.

title: Immigration by the numbers - the data sets defining Trump’s deportation agenda
speaker_org_titles: Fresnoland | Investigative Post
description_clean: The Trump administration is setting records when it comes to immigration enforcement. Record numbers of people in detention, deportations to third countries, arrests and no shows in court, money invested in deportations and clashes with U.S. citizens. Immigration reporters have been combing the numbers to create their own datasets, and resourcing other tracking organizations, to decipher the full picture of what a mass deportation plan looks like, costs, and reveals about the immigration enforcement system. Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

title: Evolving narratives in audio storytelling about crime
speakers: Brian Howey; Najib Aminy; Phillip Martin
speaker_org_titles: Free-lance Investigative Reporter  | Reporter and producer, Reveal  | The Bell Project
description_clean: Journalists hate the term "true crime." But true crime podcasts have captivated audiences, and the genre is evolving beyond its traditional confines. This panel explores how investigative journalists and storytellers are redefining true crime by focusing on systemic issues, deeper narratives and ethical storytelling. Panelists will share their experiences crafting compelling audio that goes beyond "whodunit," balancing audience engagement with sensitivity, accuracy and accountability. Attendees will gain insights into innovative reporting techniques, narrative structure and the ethical dilemmas of audio storytelling in this increasingly popular medium.

title: I-team 2.0: How to build labs pairing vets and newcomers to fill the void left by newsroom cuts
speakers: Chris Davis; Joe Stephens; Kathryn Hurd
speaker_org_titles: Deputy Editor, New York Times Local Investigative Reporting Fellowship | Executive Editor, Investigative Reporting Workshop & Professor at American University  | Senior reporter, UC Berkeley's Investigative Reporting Program
description_clean: Investigative labs increasingly fill gaps left by massive newsroom cuts. Many successfully pair experienced muckrakers with students or early-career investigators. But each lab takes a slightly different approach. Hear top editors compare and contrast styles, and explain how they assign roles, manage egos and win Pulitzers.

title: Why you should care about sports betting, even if you don't cover sports
speaker_org_titles: Data Reporter, Howard Center for Investigative Journalism @ UMD
description_clean: This session will cover the basics of proposition betting and will include a demo of our effort to capture the scope and nature of those bets during March Madness. We’ll go over what data is available and how even non-sports reporters can use it. You’ll leave this session with a working knowledge of how prop betting works and ideas about how to incorporate it into your beat.

title: Video show & tell: Anything goes with Gilbert Corsey
speaker_org_titles: WDRB News
description_clean: Sign up to “Show & Tell” us about your best work and get constructive feedback from the moderator and audience. Sign-ups are required for presenters. Each presenter will have 15 minutes to share their work and receive feedback. All are welcome to attend! Sign up here: https://www.signupgenius.com/go/10C084CA8AC29A4FBCE9-64417958-video *These sessions will not be recorded.

title: Deep dive: Cultivating sources on the inside
speaker_org_titles: Bloomberg | The New York Times
description_clean: Hamed Aleaziz and Jason Leopold have worked their way deep inside seemingly impenetrable institutions, from ICE to the Treasury Department. Their sourcing has underpinned work that’s been honored as Pulitzer finalists, including Aleaziz’s beat reporting on immigration and Leopold’s reporting on a trove of secret government records that showed dirty money coursing through major banks. In this conversation, they’ll chat about their different approaches to getting people to talk. **To allow the panelists to be candid, this session will be OFF THE RECORD - NO RECORDING/SOCIAL MEDIA POSTING**

title: Media law for journalists: Libel & invasion of privacy
speaker_org_titles: Executive Director, Media Law Resource Center
description_clean: In this session, you’ll learn about the fundamentals of libel and privacy laws applicable to journalistic content. We’ll discuss what the plaintiff has to prove to make out a libel case, your common law and constitutional defenses, the differences in defending cases brought by public and private figures, and trouble spots such as libelous implications, use of confidential sources and the value of retractions. Lawsuits for publishing truthful but embarrassing private facts will also be considered.

title: Using court records to break stories
speaker_org_titles: Court Watch/New York Times
description_clean: PACER, the federal court records system, is antiquated and hard to navigate. Using skills developed over years, Seamus Hughes has helped the media break national stories about topics ranging from terrorism and public corruption to corporate espionage. Seamus Hughes is back to give us tips and suggestions on how to use PACER to tell a story.

title: IRE Awards Luncheon
description_clean: At the IRE Awards luncheon, the 2025 IRE Awards will be presented. A salute to some of the best investigative work of the past year. In addition, the Ring of Honor inductees will be recofnized along with the Don Bolles Medal and the Golden Padlock award. Conference name badges are required for entry. A ticket to the luncheon must be purchased beforehand. Prices vary depending on your membership status. Purchase your ticket through your membership profile.

title: Adjacent Event: Journalists Recovery Network
description_clean: The Journalists Recovery Network will meet and offer a sober space for journalists experiencing troubles with alcohol or substance use. This is an informal meeting for individuals who may struggle with substance use to meet our members and chat more about our organization. Feel free to reach out to us at journalistsrecoverynetwork@gmail.com.

title: LGBTQ+ Data Reporting and Visualization
speaker_org_titles: Editor in Chief, LOOKOUT | Independent Journalist
description_clean: At a moment when misinformation, anti-LGBTQ+ laws, and attacks on trans people are all on the rise, it is crucial for journalists to find and collect critically needed data and tell stories that cover the LGBTQ+ community fairly and accurately. In this session, learn about how to work with key data (and work around limitations like undercounts of gender-nonconforming people) to create visualizations, find underreported stories, and power impactful investigations.

title: Google Sheets: Advanced pivot tables
description_clean: You've done a few pivot tables and are getting curious about what more you could do with them. What happens if you aggregate by more than one column? What are those "column" and "filter" boxes for? Come unlock the full potential of pivot tables in this intermediate spreadsheet class. This session is good for: People familiar with spreadsheets and aggregating data with pivot tables, or anyone who has taken Sheets 1-3. You must bring your own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class, and you will need a free Google account to participate.

title: Turn your questions into data with an LLM
speakers: Alexia Fernandez Campbell; Gary Harki; Mackenzie Mays
speaker_org_titles: Investigations Editor, Bloomberg Law | Investigative Reporter, Bloomberg Law | Investigative reporter, Bloomberg Law
description_clean: Learn how journalists at Bloomberg Industry Group used a large language model to turn questions into data as part of investigations into federal civil rights cases involving restraints, babies being born in jail and children being strip searched at school. This session is good for people with some experience working with data. Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training. You will also need to sign up for a ChatGPT account to participate in the hands-on exercise.

title: Deep dive: Empathy as an indispensable part of investigative reporting
speaker_org_titles: The Washington Post
description_clean: Robert Samuels, a national enterprise reporter for the Washington Post, has written about poverty, refugees, systemic racism—subjects, all, that center people confronting forces that can be unrelenting and inhumane. He co-authored “His Name Is George Floyd,” winner of the Pulitzer Prize for general nonfiction. Lizzie Presser, a reporter on ProPublica’s national staff, has written about racial disparities in amputations and dispossession of Black landowners in the South. Her work on preventable deaths following state abortion bans won the Pulitzer Prize for public service. For both, a signature of their powerful, considerate work is empathy.

title: Resources for local news investigations
speakers: Kim Kleman; Rose Ciotta; Sarah Blustain; Sona Patel
speaker_org_titles: Assistant Managing Editor, Local, ProPublica | Executive Director, Investigative Editing Corps | Executive Director, Report for America  | Program and Editorial Director, Local Investigations Fellowship, The New York Times
description_clean: Come hear from organizations that are providing front-line help to local newsrooms: Report for America's partnership with Investigative Editing Corps; New York Times's Local Investigations Fellowship and ProPublica's Local Reporting Fellowship. You will leave this session with information on what each program can mean for you and your newsroom, and how you can fine-tune a winning pitch.

title: Investigative podcast series: ambitious storytelling and winning collaborations
speakers: Adelina Lancianese; Alison MacAdam; Meribah Knight
speaker_org_titles: Freelance story editor | Nashville Public Radio / Independent | Senior Producer, NPR's Embedded Podcast
description_clean: When investigative reporters collaborate with a narrative audio team, podcast magic gets made. But how do these collaborations even begin? How do you keep a sprawling – sometimes year-long – production process on track? And why is a podcast series the right fit for your investigation? The Embedded podcast, NPR’s home for documentary storytelling, thrives on a model of collaboration. You’ll hear Embedded producers, editors, and reporters explain how they turn in-depth reporting into award-winning documentaries like Embedded’s Supermajority and Tested. Plus, they’ll share tips for making immersive, fact-based podcast series that keep listeners hooked from start to finish.

title: Teaching the investigation: how students can do groundbreaking investigative stories
speaker_org_titles: Mongabay
description_clean: Investigations done at universities are not only teaching the next generation, they are providing valuable stories for communities. Uncovering dangerous day cares. Exposing police shootings. Uncovering legislators living lavishly. Identifying dangerous railroad bridges. Revealing officer misconduct.

title: Debt collection USA: How to cover surging debt collection lawsuits near you
speakers: Frederick Wherry; Jenifer McKim; Noam N. Levey
speaker_org_titles: KFF Health News | Princeton University | Senior editor, Investigations and Equity and Justice
description_clean: The number of debt collection lawsuits are rising nationwide, propelled by debt-buyers, credit card companies and a wide array of industries from hospitals to mortgage companies to solar panels. Our panelists will provide on-the-ground examples and data tips on how to mine courts for all kinds of stories affecting people struggling with consumer debt.

title: Investigating the child welfare system
speakers: Clara Bates; Eli Hager; Laura Bennett; Roxanna Asgarian
speaker_org_titles: Director, The Center for Just Journalism | Journalist and Author | Reporter, ProPublica | Reporter, Santa Fe New Mexican
description_clean: Across the US, government agencies investigate millions of families each year through what is commonly referred to as the child welfare system. Yet much of this activity functions as a form of state surveillance and intervention that operates with limited transparency and public understanding. Coverage of the system often relies on official narratives about child safety without examining the systemic factors - including poverty, housing instability, and service gaps - that drive involvement in the system or the impact of investigations and separations on children and families. This panel will focus on practical strategies for investigating the functioning of the child welfare system. Panelists will share how to obtain and interpret agency data, analyze removal and reporting patterns, and identify stories that go beyond individual tragedies to examine policy and structural issues. Panelists will also discuss sourcing challenges, records barriers, and ethical considerations when reporting on families and children.

title: Deep dive: The reporter and editor relationship
speaker_org_titles: The New Yorker
description_clean: Jane Mayer is one of the best-known investigative journalists in the country, having covered the Bush administration’s torture program, the dark money flowing through politics, and Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth’s secret history. Yet even she needs an editor. Dan Zalewski has edited Mayer’s work for decades. He is known at the New Yorker for making reporters’ work sing and pushing them to achieve the seemingly impossible. In this conversation, they’ll talk through how to make the most of the reporter and editor relationship.

title: From the idea to the prototype with Google AI Tools
speaker_org_titles: Trainer, Google News Initiative
description_clean: In this hands-on workshop we will do a deep dive into Google’s AI tools —including NotebookLM, and Gemini, among others—designed specifically for complex news workflows. We’ll move from prompting 101 to developing simple, functional prototypes and custom research agents you can deploy immediately. Prefer to learn by observing? You are completely welcome to just come and watch the build process! Pre-registration is required and seating is limited. Participants must have a Google account, as well as Gemini and NotebookLM.

title: Master the power of mapping with QGIS
speaker_org_titles: Senior Editor of Data Journalism at The Atlanta Journal-Constitution | UC Berkeley School of Journalism
description_clean: Mapping is a powerful tool to find patterns in data that can only be explored on a map. This session will give you the basics for doing mapping with the open-source tool QGIS. You'll learn how to build thematic maps, how to bring outside data into QGIS and connect it to your map and how to combine data in a map to look at geographic relationships in your data. This class is good for those with solid spreadsheet skills ready to move into mapping. You will need a laptop. Please download and install the long-term release of QGIS for your operating system (3.44.10 Solothurn LTR) at qgis.org/download. Please note that the LTR is not the latest release, but the most current stable release. Pre-registration is required and seating is limited.

title: Office Hours: 1:1 career meetings with newsroom leaders & hiring managers
speaker_org_titles: Kimbap Media
description_clean: Looking for specific feedback on a cover letter or portfolio piece? Maybe you want to wordsmith a section of your resume or update your bio. Sign up for a 20-min one-on-one session with a manager or newsroom leader with expertise in data, editing, investigations, career pathways and more. I would like to coordinate this program again, similar to NICAR! It would be awesome to get a ballroom with 6-10 tables rather than sending folks to random hallway corners, OR a series of small meeting rooms (1-2 mentor coaches per room, etc). My goal would be to set up TWO to THREE hours worth of timeslots with 8-12 mentor coaches, and a goal of registering 50-75 people. I marked masterclass below, just cos this definitely needs a bigger timeslot than a panel or workshop.

title: 15 min break
Bringing data and records to life in audio
speaker_org_titles: Independent Investigative Journalist | Investigative data reporter for USA Today
description_clean: The data that journalists work with is often messy, and they rarely provide us with clear-cut or sweeping conclusions. How can we bring complicated numbers, especially in finance and science, to the listeners in a way that makes sense? How can we unearth public recordings that are often never heard? Panelists will share their strategies for breaking down complex concepts, finding the human stories behind the data, and crafting compelling audio narratives with public records.

title: Google Sheets 1: Getting started with spreadsheets (repeat)
description_clean: In this introduction to spreadsheets, you'll begin analyzing data with Google Sheets, a simple but powerful tool. You'll learn how to enter data, navigate spreadsheets and conduct simple calculations like sum, average and median. This session is good for: Data beginners. You must bring your own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class, and you will need a free Google account to participate.

title: Charts for small newsrooms
speaker_org_titles: Elections & Investigations Reporter, Central Florida Public Media
description_clean: Charts for small newsrooms Just because you're in a small newsroom doesn't mean you can't make great, informative charts! Even a little bit of Excel can take you far. In this session, we'll show you how to quickly make a great graphic by yourself, using publicly available election numbers to make simple but informative graphics. We'll also go over some principles of good graphic design to make sure your charts are communicating what they need to, and how to export charts for print or web publication. Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

title: Protecting your sources, devices, and work in a new era of press scrutiny
AI tools you can use
speakers: Cheryl Phillips; Dylan Freedman; Sef Kloninger
speaker_org_titles: Big Local News Founder/Lecturer, Stanford University | Engineer, Big Local News, Stanford University | The New York Times

title: Building blocks of leadership
speaker_org_titles: President & CEO of Radio Television Digital News Association (RTDNA)
description_clean: Is your team clicking together like a perfectly built structure or clunking along like mismatched pieces? Strong collaboration and communication are the foundation of any high-performing team, and in this interactive session, you’ll discover how to strengthen both through the power of play. Using building blocks as both metaphor and hands-on tool, we’ll explore what it takes to build trust, align around shared goals and adapt to change. You’ll gain fresh insights into team dynamics, leadership styles and the invisible structures that support effective teamwork. Come ready to build, reflect and leave with practical strategies for assembling a more connected, resilient team: one brick at a time.

title: Behind the story on the accessibility beat
speakers: Ainsley Martinez; Eli Cahan; Emilia Ruzicka
speaker_org_titles: Independent Journalist | Journalist / Physician, Rolling Stone / Stanford University | NYC Local Editor, Patch

title: Scams and scammers: Investigating the fastest-growing crime in the world
speakers: Juliet Linderman; Martha Mendoza; Spencer Woodman
speaker_org_titles: FRONTLINE/PBS | Investigative reporter, The Associated Press | The International Consortium of Investigative Journalists
description_clean: Scam attempts are no longer poorly spelled missives from a prince in west Africa. Today one of the largest crimes in the world is showing up in text messages, popping onto social media, ripping off friends and neighbors. This session equips investigative journalists with practical methods to identify, document, and expose modern scam ecosystems. Drawing on real reporting examples, (and there’s one in every town), we’ll walk through how to spot early warning signs, emotional manipulation methods, digital footprints, follow money flows, and try to connect individual victims to larger fraud networks through blockchain analysis. Attendees will learn how to use open-source tools, court records, platform data, and victim interviews to build airtight investigations; how to safely engage with scammers without compromising security; and how to collaborate across newsrooms and borders when scams scale internationally. The session will also address ethical and legal considerations, including source protection, digital safety, and avoiding re-victimization. Designed for reporters at any stage of experience, this workshop focuses on turning scattered complaints into high-impact accountability journalism that exposes who profits, who enables the fraud, and why regulators and platforms so often fail to stop it.

title: Investigating the aftermath of climate disasters
speakers: Brianna Sacks; Felicia Sonmez; Megan Munce; Zach Despart
speaker_org_titles: Climate Reporter, San Francisco Chronicle | Disaster correspondent; The Washington Post | Growth and Development Reporter, Blue Ridge Public Radio | Reporter, ProPublica
description_clean: The last few years have demonstrated the devastation of climate change-fueled disasters. While much reporting is focused on the immediate aftermath of these disasters, resources and attention fade quickly. But it's in the months and years after a disaster where investigative journalism is the most crucial -- examining whether systems set up to support recovery are truly working and what can be done to prepare for the next natural disaster. This panel brings together reporters who have covered wildfires in Los Angeles and Maui, hurricanes in Florida and North Carolina and flooding in Texas to discuss building trust with sources who have experienced trauma, finding accountability angles in a rapidly developing situation and tackle emerging issues around safety and recovery given the ever evolving nature of climate disasters.

title: Fact-checking for reporters
speakers: Darya Marchenkova; Mariam Elba; Nina Zweig
speaker_org_titles: Independent fact-checker and investigative researcher | Research Editor, Type Investigations | Research Reporter, ProPublica
description_clean: Most newsrooms can no longer afford to spend resources on fact checking, leaving them exposed to potential corrections, public embarrassment, or even litigation. Given the current political climate and the administration's aggressive stance toward watchdog journalism, it's more important than ever to learn how to vet your own work and protect yourself. This session, led by Type Investigations Research Editor Nina Zweig, will teach reporters how to self-check their work and approach their reporting with an eye toward fact checking.

title: 15 min break
Networking: Women
description_clean: Mix and mingle, meet friends old and new, and build your professional community in this fun and informal networking session. This session is for people who identify as women.

title: Google Sheets 2: Formulas & sorting (repeat)
description_clean: Much of Google Sheets' power comes in the form of formulas. In this class, you'll learn how to use them to analyze data with the eye of a journalist. Yes, math will be involved, but it's totally worth it! This class will show you how calculations like change, percent change, rates and ratios can beef up your reporting. This session is good for: Anyone who has taken Google Sheets 1 or has been introduced to spreadsheets. You must bring your own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class, and you will need a free Google account to participate.

title: Finding the story: Covering public utilities
speaker_org_titles: Environment and Data Reporter, St. Louis Public Radio | Investigative Reporter - AfroLA
description_clean: Public utility coverage state regulatory agencies were historically sleepy and obscure. But now, they're at the center of some of the most contentious issues journalists cover, including data centers, utility affordability and climate change. Wildfires, AI, climate change, healthcare, and affordability crisis coverage could all be improved with knowledge about how public utilities -- particularly water and power utilities operate. This hands-on session will share potential storylines and datasets to explore to find your next scoop.

title: When video broke open ICE investigations
speakers: Kelly Dietz; Stacey Cameron; Yamilet Virgin
speaker_org_titles: Investigative Reporter - WSMV4 Nashville | KABB WOAI San Antonio, Investigative Reporter/Anchor | KARE 11, Investigative producer
description_clean: From citizen deaths at the hands of federal agents to fast-paced detentions and deportations carried out without due process, video has become one of the most powerful tools journalists have to investigate immigration enforcement. In this panel, reporters covering ICE in their own communities will discuss how they find and verify video to break news, use it to challenge narratives, and report responsibly when footage is the only evidence that exists—especially when it contradicts the government’s account. Panelists will also share strategies for protecting vulnerable and undocumented sources and uncovering what's happening in neighborhoods across the country as human stories, not just national headlines.

title: Break into prison coverage: How to report on federal prisons, no matter your beat
speakers: Kaley Johnson; Keri Blakinger; Mike Balsamo
speaker_org_titles: Freelance Reporter | ProPublica Reporter | US News Director, The Associated Press
description_clean: This session will cover how to report on federal prisons. Many outlets’ coverage areas include or intersect with federal prisons, but not all outlets have reporters who cover incarceration exclusively. Federal prisons can also be intimidating to reporters of all experience-levels; they are a black box of bureaucratic tape and obstacles. The goal of this panel is to teach reporters and editors across a spectrum of beats and experience level how to incorporate federal prison coverage into their newsroom.

title: Investigative stories you can feel: Engaging audiences through voices
speakers: Katie Colaneri; Mark Greenblatt; Robert Cribb; Susanne Reber
speaker_org_titles: Director, Investigative Journalism Bureau | Executive Producer, Piz Gloria Productions | Howard Center for Investigative Journalism ASU | Senior Editor of Podcasts, NHPR
description_clean: How can investigative journalism create lasting impact and resonate with audiences. This panel explores how to craft stories that reveal deep truths while keeping listeners engaged.This panel explores the art of navigating liminal spaces, storytelling that exists as both hard-hitting exposé and character-driven narrative, and what it means to bring the panelists own histories and perspectives to an investigation. What does personal point of view unlock in terms of nuance and humanity, and where do the challenges lie?

title: Digging through the archives
speakers: Alison Young; Jamie Corey; Mark Feldstein
speaker_org_titles: Global Research Director of Desmog | Senior National Reporter, Healthbeat

title: Lessons from the best international investigative and data projects
speakers: Carla Minet; Rowan Philp; Sarah Cohen; Will Fitzgibbon
speaker_org_titles: Centro de Periodismo Investigativo | Global Investigative Journalism Network | Reporter, The Examination | Sr Data Reporter, Big Local News
description_clean: Now host to both the Global Shining Light Award - recognizing investigations from perilous places – and the SIGMA global data journalism award, GIJN recently announced the 4 winners for the former, and 10 for the latter. In this session, judges, organizers and winners of those competitions will share some of the methodologies that inspired them, and takeaways from these foreign stories that could benefit US journalists.

title: Structuring successful partnerships: Professional newsrooms and university investigative programs
speaker_org_titles: Howard Center for Investigative Journalism | Howard Center for Investigative Journalism ASU
description_clean: University investigative journalism program leaders and their professional newsroom collaborators will talk about how to build successful partnerships to publish Pulitzer-finalist-quality investigative projects produced by students and pros. Attendees will walk away with concrete tips on how to develop and manage collaborations between experienced professionals and early-career reporters.

title: Covering power when power pushes back: lessons from international journalists
speakers: Anna Nemzer; Clavel Rangel; Mahir Zeynalov; Paul Haven
speaker_org_titles: Associated Press | Globe Post Media | Independent Journalist, Knight Wallace Fellow 26' | Kronika
description_clean: Across the world, journalists have spent years reporting under governments that intimidate reporters, surveil sources, weaponize legal systems, and attempt to discredit independent media. As political hostility toward the press intensifies in the United States, investigative reporters can learn valuable strategies from colleagues who have long navigated these pressures. This panel, organized by the North American Committee of the International Press Institute, a 75-year-old free press organization, explores what American investigative reporters and accountability journalists can learn from colleagues who have worked under sustained government pressure around the world. Panelists with firsthand experience covering authoritarian or semi-authoritarian governments will share lessons learned from those environments—including techniques for cultivating government sources, protecting sensitive communications, anticipating legal threats, and continuing accountability reporting when officials attempt to intimidate journalists or their sources. The panel will focus on practical reporting techniques and source-protection strategies reporters can immediately apply in their own investigative work. Attendees will leave with concrete strategies used by journalists around the world to keep reporting—even when governments push back.

title: IRE Membership Meeting
description_clean: Open to all IRE members.

title: Recharge Room
description_clean: Need a spot to decompress, take a load off, get your bearings or recharge your batteries? This is the room for you!

title: Registration Hours
description_clean: Pick up your badge or register on-site: 8:00 a.m. - 2:30 p.m.

title: Adjacent Event: Journalists Recovery Network
description_clean: The Journalists Recovery Network will meet and offer a sober space for journalists experiencing troubles with alcohol or substance use. This is an informal meeting for individuals who may struggle with substance use to meet our members and chat more about our organization. Feel free to reach out to us at journalistsrecoverynetwork@gmail.com.

title: Exhibitors
description_clean: Stop by and visit with the exhibitors and recruiters.

title: Barry Nash and Company One-on-One Coaching
description_clean: Barry Nash and his team return to IRE to provide personalized coaching sessions. If you have questions about any aspect of your delivery — your appearance, how you use your voice, how you can perform most effectively in the field or in the studio — this is an opportunity you won’t want to miss. Barry has been working with talent at all levels of experience and ability for over 30 years. He currently authors the only blog in the world focused exclusively on television news and sports performance. You can find it on Substack Please note: Participants should bring recent footage on a flash drive, webpage/online link, or storage drive (Google or Dropbox). Sign up for a time slot here: https://www.signupgenius.com/go/10C0445A9AD28A5FEC34-64383836-irecoaching#/

title: Audio Coaching 1:1
description_clean: Sign up for 30 minutes with an audio pro to use however you like: Bring tape for a critique, a pitch for workshopping or your questions about surviving and thriving in the audio journalism space.

title: Finding the story: Housing data
speaker_org_titles: Bankrate | El Tecolote
description_clean: This hands-on session will teach you how to find your next housing story in data. You'll learn a practical method for predicting metro-area home price trends using Census building permit and population data. Additionally, you'll take a look at towing data and learn how it affects housing. Whether you're covering displacement, gentrification, or the affordable housing crisis, this session will equip you with datasets and techniques to investigate how cities are quietly reshaping who belongs in their communities. This session is good for beginners. You must bring your own laptop (no tablets) to participate.

title: Making news archives more useful with LLMs
speakers: Cat Murphy; Derek Willis; Ijeoma Opara
speaker_org_titles: Data Reporter, Howard Center for Investigative Journalism @ UMD | Howard Center for Investigative Journalism | University of Maryland
description_clean: Some of the most valuable information news organizations have sits in their archives, mostly unused. New reporters or those just new to a beat need to know the collected wisdom from past coverage, and Large Language Models can help make that process easier. This session covers the concept of employing artificial intelligence to harness the potential of news archives and shows off some experiments with local news organizations. Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

title: Making the most of OTT: How to make your investigative content stand out on streaming services
speaker_org_titles: Chief Investigator/Anchor, KSLA News 12
description_clean: Investigative pieces by video journalists are no longer limited to a TRT of just a few minutes. Thanks to streaming services, reporters and producers are able to use their newsroom’s OTT platforms to tell longer and more in-depth stories, or to take audiences behind the scenes of how their investigations are made. But with so much OTT content available, how do successful journalists attract people to their work? In this session, reporters and producers share unique insights about how they’ve leveraged OTT platforms to effectively broaden their reach.

title: Beating burnout at its foundation
speaker_org_titles: Chief Rebellion Officer for Rebellion Collective
description_clean: Burnout isn’t just about overworking — it’s about broken systems that force you to do everything yourself. Learn how to build burnout-proof businesses with custom systems, automation, and smarter workflows so your business runs smoothly — without running you into the ground.

title: How to make yourself a better writer
speaker_org_titles: Investigative Reporter, Bloomberg News | New York Times Opinion deputy editor for Ideas and Investigations
description_clean: What separates a solid draft from a story that truly sings? In this interactive session, Ariel Kaminer, who has edited everything from narrative features to deep-dive investigations to M. Gessen's Pulitzer Prize winning opinion writing, will take you behind the scenes on her process. She'll share her techniques for sharpening structure, tightening language, bringing characters to life, elevating scenes, and finding the strongest version of a story. Kaminer is one of those editors who seems to possess a magical scalpel. After she’s done with a reporter’s draft, she has made it sharper, clearer, more propulsive, and more readable but somehow left enough of the original style on the page that it's actually possible for a reporter to think "YES, I wrote that!" (Kendall has been subjected to the Ariel treatment and can vouch for it). She'll also lead a live editing session using small chunks of participants' writing and talk through how to self-edit your work. You'll be left with concrete tools to help make your writing shine. For those who would like to submit work for editing, please send 1-5 sentences max from an unpublished or published story to Kendall (kendall.taggart.journalist@gmail.com).

title: Juggling print and audio investigations (and how to do both, very different things at the same time)
speakers: Aliyya Swaby; Ashley Cleek; Hannah Levintova; Paige Pfleger
speaker_org_titles: Producer at CIR | ProPublica reporter | Senior Criminal Justice Reporter, WPLN News | Special Projects Editor & Reporter at Mother Jones
description_clean: Collaborations between print and audio journalists have generated chart-topping, change-making, award-winning podcasts, vastly magnifying the reach of investigative stories. These collaborations have the power to bring stories to life, take listeners inside the reporting process, and explore universal themes unearthed in the reporting. But the difference between print and audio storytelling isn't just stylistic; Sometimes these mediums have different aims altogether, and these collaborations force journalists to contend with diverging ideas of "impact" and other questions. In this panel, you'll hear from two pairs of print and audio journalists who successfully navigated these crosswinds to create collaborations that have resonated nationally.

title: Open mic - Generation Gap, or not?
speakers: Daniela Ibarra; Olivia Reed; Scott Zamost
speaker_org_titles: CNBC Senior Investigative Producer | Investigative Reporter, KSAT | University of Nebraska-Lincoln
description_clean: Seasoned and early-career journalists lead a discussion about what we can learn from each other. Let's have an open and honest discussion about being new, mid-career and seasoned in the industry. This open mic will discuss the challenges we face and how we can all learn from each other to make the industry better.

title: Digging into public companies with SEC documents
speaker_org_titles: McGraw Center for Business Journalism  | Professor, Northwestern University/Medill
description_clean: Documents from the SEC (U.S Securities and Exchange Commission) contains a wealth of information to power your business investigations. In this panel, you'll learn about using those SEC and AI tools to find DEI commitments, and others, that public companies have quietly rolled back.

title: Investigating global health crises that hit home
speakers: Adam Rhodes; Andy Pierrotti; Susan Ferriss; Zhe Wu
speaker_org_titles: Investigative Reporter, WANF-TV | Investigative Reporters & Editors, Training Director and Managing Editor  | Pulitzer Center on Crisis Reporting, Senior Editor  | Reporter, The San Francisco Public Press
description_clean: There’s no shortage of health stories to investigate. The country is dealing with fallout from measles outbreaks, deep cuts to health care access, and misinformation about vaccine safety and other established health facts. Beyond the U.S., the abrupt “America First” demolition of USAID and withdrawal from the World Health Organization threaten gains against HIV and other diseases that cross borders. This panel will explore investigating health crises that address misinformation and link local communities to the rest of the world.

title: Covering state courts: unlocking the legal system
speakers: Emily Saul; Frank G. Runyeon; Jeremy Chase
speaker_org_titles: ALM Media and the New York Law Journal | Partner, Media and Intellectual Property at Davis Wright Tremaine LLP | Senior Reporter, Law360 & President, Courthouse Reporters of New York
description_clean: Over 98% of the U.S. court cases are found in state courthouses, where much of the country’s justice —and injustice— occurs. They're chronically under-covered compared to federal courts. But esoteric terms, archaic filing systems and unfriendly court staff can hamper access to a treasure trove of unmined stories. In this session, you’ll learn tips and tricks to navigate your local courts and a media law expert will show you how to fight for your First Amendment right to access documents and courtroom proceedings if anyone tries to (unlawfully) stop you.

title: Managing investigators: Leading those born to challenge authority (10th Edition)
speakers: Cindy Galli; Emma Carew Grovum; Gonzalo Magana; Jamie Grey; Jennifer Forsyth; Jodie Fleischer; Josh Hinkle; Kat Stafford; Mc Nelly Torres; Nicole Vap
speaker_org_titles: ABC10/TEGNA, Director of Special Projects and Engagment  | Cox Media Group, Managing Editor of Investigative Content and Collaborations | Director of Investigations & Innovation - KXAN | Director, Justice and Legal Reporting, MS NOW | Independent Journalist  | Independent Journalist, editor & forthcoming book author | InvestigateTV/Gray Media Director of Investigations | Kimbap Media | Reuters, Global Race and Justice Editor | The New York Times, Washington Investigations Editor
description_clean: Being a news manager is already tough, but what if you supervise investigative journalists? They come with an extra layer of challenges because their very job (and likely their personality) makes them hyper-alert to authority figures. This course is designed to give you some tools and tactics to lead individuals and entire teams of investigators more effectively. Learn from three investigative managers from different media at different stages of their leadership careers. How did they launch into their roles, and what experience have they gained along the way? This course is for current investigative managers and anyone aspiring to step into such a position in the future. NEW AT IRE26: For our 10th "Managing Investigators" master class (and our 50th IRE Conference), we're bringing back a BIG group of our past instructors for even more guidance, viewpoints and support! Think of it as Managing Investigators 2.0! More individual time and leaders for coaching! Topics will include: managing compassionately, hiring challenges, transitioning to management, forging partnerships, building relationships, handling resource cuts, organization/structure, tough decisions/conversations, in-house training/growth, delivering feedback, creating inclusive opportunities, and juggling responsibilities/projects/work. Pre-registration is required and seating is limited.

title: Art of the investigative interview
speakers: A.J. Lagoe; Cheryl W. Thompson; Mark Horvit
speaker_org_titles: Investigative Correspondent, NPR | KARE 11 | University of Missouri
description_clean: You need one final confirmation to run your story. It's the key piece of a months-long project, only one person can provide it, and you've got one shot to get it. The entire story comes down to The Interview. Will you be ready? We can help. Sign up for this half-day Master Class in interviewing and get a playbook for getting the information you need. We'll cover all the crucial steps, from preparation to the conversation, confrontational interviews, talking with survivors, getting the most from witnesses, convincing those who don't want to talk with you and much more. So the next time you dial that number, knock on that door or corner an official who’s been avoiding you, you’ll be ready. Preregistration is required and seating is limited.

title: The paper trail: Finding local stories in national ICE arrest, detainer and detention data
speakers: Aline Behar Kado; Raphael Romero Ruiz
speaker_org_titles: Howard Fellow, Howard Center for Investigative Journalism | University of Maryland
description_clean: Millions of records. One enforcement surge. And somewhere in it all, your story. The Deportation Data Project‘s FOIA-obtained releases on ICE arrests, detentions, and detainers are among the most detailed public records ever produced on American immigration enforcement — and in this workshop, you’ll learn how to work them in R. We’ll clean, join, and interrogate all three datasets to trace the enforcement pipeline from detainer request to detention facility, surfacing the patterns and outliers that make national data locally meaningful. You‘ll leave with working code, a reproducible workflow, and story angles you can start reporting when you get back to your newsrooms. Basic R familiarity required. Pre-registration is required and seating is limited.

title: 15 min break
Your AI research assistant: a live demo for investigative reporters
speaker_org_titles: Senior Editor of Data Journalism at The Atlanta Journal-Constitution
description_clean: AI tools like ChatGPT and Claude can do a lot more than write emails and summarize articles. In this live demo, we'll put them to work on the kinds of tasks that eat up your reporting time: pulling data off a web page without writing a scraper, turning a messy PDF into a structured spreadsheet, comparing two versions of a bill or contract to find what changed, cleaning and standardizing inconsistent names or addresses, and building a quick local app to browse and search your documents. No slides, no theory. It's just a fast-paced walkthrough of real investigative tasks, with time for "can it do this?" questions from the audience.

title: Mistakes we learned from and you can too!
speakers: Chris Vanderveen; Stephen Stock; Tanya Eiserer; Tracee Wilkins
speaker_org_titles: Assistant News Director/Investigative Journalist | KUSA-TV Investigative Reporter  | NBC Washington, Investigative Reporter | Senior investigative reporter WFAA-TV
description_clean: We all have success stories in investigative journalism, but it's the mistakes we make along the way that truly impact our careers for the better. These journalists share honest experiences in how they overcome them and what those missteps can teach us all.

title: ICE, HHS, EOIR: Covering the alphabet soup of immigration agencies
speakers: Doug MacMillan; Lomi Kriel; Nicole Foy; Ximena Bustillo
speaker_org_titles: DHS + Immigration Correspondent | Investigative Reporter | ProPublica investigative reporter | The Washington Post
description_clean: Immigration policy has always played out across several federal agencies: Homeland Security, Labor, Health and Human Service, Justice and more. But now the Trump administration’s whole of government approach to a mass deportation agenda means everyone plays some part as agents from the FBI, DEA, ATF, the U.S. Marshals, and even the Coast Guard or the Secret Service are increasingly making street-level arrests. Join journalists covering federal agencies for a session on how the agencies they cover have played a role and how to navigate them all on deadline

title: Audio meetup
speaker_org_titles: Retired Correspondent, NPR Investigations; Chair, Governing Board, Public Health Watch | Reveal from The Center for Investigative Reporting
description_clean: Whether you work in the audio space or just want to get to know the people who do, come by for a casual meet and greet with friends and colleagues.

tracks: Beat reporting; Local news; Story ideas
title: Investigating rail safety with data and docs
speakers: Aaron Leibowitz; Cat Murphy; Haley Parsley; Lex Doig; Trish Wilson Belli
speaker_org_titles: Data Reporter, Howard Center for Investigative Journalism @ UMD | Investigations Editor | Reporter, Miami Herald | University of Maryland
description_clean: Learn how to investigate rail safety in your community from reporters on two award-winning projects, including a 2025 IRE winner. They will share public data, records, sources and strategies for documenting hidden risks and deadly hazards.

title: The global use of freelancers for investigative reporting
speaker_org_titles: Director, Freelance Investigative Reporters and Editors (FIRE)
description_clean: What is the public-interest potential of the freelance investigative reporting sector, to what extent is it being realized? What are the variables? To what extent, and how, could a robust freelance investigative reporting sector offset some of the economic upheaval and hollowing out — locally, regionally, nationally, globally. How could they supplement small nonprofit newsrooms, for example? Interact with them?

title: Video show & tell: en Español with Carolina Cruz and Liz Gonzalez
speaker_org_titles: Consumer Investigative Reporter Telemundo 47 NY
description_clean: Sign up to “Show & Tell” us about your best work and get constructive feedback from the moderator and audience. Sign-ups are required for presenters. Each presenter will have 15 minutes to share their work and receive feedback. All are welcome to attend! Sign up here: https://www.signupgenius.com/go/10C084CA8AC29A4FBCE9-64417544-video *These sessions will not be recorded.

title: Follow that supply chain! Uncover how questionable products end up on store shelves
speakers: Agatha Khishchenko; Andrew Lehren; Katie McQue; Robert Cribb
speaker_org_titles: CUNY Graduate School of Journalism | Director, Investigative Journalism Bureau | Freelance investigative reporter  | Mongabay
description_clean: Our planet is increasing global. Furniture from Vietnam. Cashews from Cambodia. Oysters from Vancouver. Clothing from Indonesia. Unscrupulous companies involved in environmental destruction or labor abuses may be behind brand-name goods. This panel will help you follow the supply chain for your investigation.

title: Using AI video tools to keep local government accountable
speaker_org_titles: Howard Center for Investigative Journalism
description_clean: Thousands of local government meetings happen every week, and most newsrooms can only cover a fraction of them. A new wave of AI-powered tools is helping journalists keep tabs on far more meetings than any one reporter could track alone — transcribing recordings, identifying key moments and flagging potentially newsworthy actions. Journalists will hear from developers at newsrooms and universities who are actively building these tools, learning how they work in practice, what they catch and what they miss, and the editorial judgment calls involved in using AI to help surface the news. For newsrooms stretched thin, the payoff is real: broader monitoring of local government frees up reporters to spend more time on the in-depth work that holds officials accountable. Attendees will leave with a clear picture of what's available now, what's coming soon, and how to evaluate whether these tools could work in their newsroom. Could be a panel or a demo. I also have several people I think would be great candidates for this who I have collaborated with I can reach out to if this session is greenlit. If someone else is doing this, I'd be happy to join their panel!

title: Building trust with sources in a time of fear and retaliation
speaker_org_titles: Executive editor and chief content officer, Deep South Today | Narrative enterprise reporter, The Washingston Post
description_clean: Washington Post reporter Hannah Natanson became known as the "federal government whisperer" for her extraordinary ability to cultivate trust with sources across the federal workforce. Using social media, encrypted Signal chats and tireless communications, sometimes fielding hundreds of Signal messages from sources a day, she built a network of more than 1,200 confidential sources. Her reporting on the far-reaching impact of the Trump administration's Department of Government Efficiency grew out of the conversations, insights and trust she built over time. In this session, Natanson will discuss how journalists can build credibility with sources, sustain trust over the long term, and protect vulnerable sources who may fear personal or professional retaliation.

title: 15 min break
Strategies for investigating offshore companies
speaker_org_titles: Freelance investigative journalist | Reuters Financial Regulation Correspondent
description_clean: This hands-on session will show you how to use publicly available information to identify who owns and benefits from companies that are set up in jurisdictions with comprehensive corporate secrecy. In an era of increasing transnational corruption, being able to publicly expose who is behind a company and the deals in which they participate will be critical for accountability journalism. While having a leak or an inside source that exposes this information would be ideal, this is not something we can rely on and there are other approaches. This training draws from years of experience piercing the veil around such secretive companies. Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

title: Google Sheets: Using string functions to manipulate data
speaker_org_titles: Spotlight PA
description_clean: Maybe you converted a PDF or imported a table into a spreadsheet -- or maybe an agency gave you a poorly formatted file. You can use string functions to reformat your data and get your spreadsheets working for you. This session is good for: Anyone comfortable with using formulas and functions in Google Sheets. You must bring your own laptop (no tablets) to participate in this class, and you will need a free Google account to participate.

title: 30 video ideas that work anywhere
speakers: Justin Lum; Katie Wilcox; Lori Jane Gliha
speaker_org_titles: Investigative Reporter & Editor | The Beam at ASU | Investigative Reporter, KSAZ | Senior National Investigative Correspondent, Scripps News
description_clean: The demand for news sometimes requires us to turn investigations fast. In 30 Ideas that work anywhere, these journalists will share true in-depth and quick turns that you can do as soon as you return to your newsroom.

title: Tips, sources and documents: How to investigate the intersection of religion, politics & culture
speakers: James Grimaldi; Liam Adams; Michelle Boorstein; Tiffany Stanley
speaker_org_titles: Associated Press | Investigative Journalist | The Tennessean | Washington Post
description_clean: Religion is taking center stage in the Trump administration and public life. Join veteran religion beat reporters and journalists who reveal ways to develop sources and find documents to investigate corruption and controversy in religious groups and uncover public and hidden connections to political campaigns and secular society. The panel will touch on diocese bankruptcies, Christian nationalist influence over the White House, and scandals in the Catholic Church and Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints. This session is presented in partnership with the Religion News Association.

title: A year in local investigations: Watchdog story ideas
speaker_org_titles: Investigative reporter, The Seattle Times | Tampa Bay Times
description_clean: Learn tricks from the stories and reporters featured in the Local Matters newsletter, which spotlights a selection of local watchdog journalism every Sunday. The journalists behind Local Matters will run through inspiring work the past year — with a focus on tactics and tips you can bring back to your own newsroom. We will feature stories from online outlets, radio, television and newspapers.

title: Editing the investigative podcast
speaker_org_titles: Reveal from The Center for Investigative Reporting | Senior Editor of Podcasts, NHPR
description_clean: Experienced editors share their best tips on deciding when to pursue long-form audio stories, developing story arcs that grip listeners and guiding your team through creating a revelatory and impactful podcast.

title: Video show & tell: Anything goes with Erik Sandoval
speaker_org_titles: Graham Media Group Director of Storytelling
description_clean: Sign up to “Show & Tell” us about your best work and get constructive feedback from the moderator and audience. Sign-ups are required for presenters. Each presenter will have 15 minutes to share their work and receive feedback. All are welcome to attend! Sign up here: https://www.signupgenius.com/go/10C084CA8AC29A4FBCE9-64418011-video *These sessions will not be recorded.

title: Rethinking the crime beat
speakers: Alaina Bookman; Cheryl Thompson-Morton; Hannah Riley Fernandez; Tim Spears
speaker_org_titles: AL.com Reporter | Director of Programming, the Center for Just Journalism | Investigative Reporter, WISH-TV | The Lenfest Institute for Journalism
description_clean: Some newsrooms are rethinking traditional crime coverage; moving beyond incident driven reporting toward more holistic public safety beats. This shift reflects both audience needs and newsroom realities: declining resources, growing public skepticism of official narratives, and recognition that issues like housing instability, substance use, behavioral health, and economic conditions all shape community safety outcomes as much, if not more, than the crimes tracked by police. This panel will highlight journalists and outlets that have successfully revamped their crime beats and explain how they did it, from a local ABC affiliate in South Bend rethinking their nightly broadcast to AL.com's 'Beyond the Violence' beat. Panelists will share practical strategies for identifying new story angles, developing sources beyond law enforcement, using data to contextualize incidents, and building trusting relationships with the communities they cover.

title: Investigating the privatization of K-12 education
speakers: Carla Minet; Jennifer Smith Richards; Joe Dana; Laura Meckler
speaker_org_titles: 12News Investigative Reporter | Centro de Periodismo Investigativo | National Education Writer, The Washington Post | ProPublica
description_clean: Publicly funded education is increasingly being offered at private schools. In Republican states across the country, sweeping school voucher programs provide taxpayer money for private school tuition and even homeschooling expenses. And in 2027, the first federal voucher program will launch, bringing vouchers to states that never had them before. Proponents tout these programs as education freedom for families, who can choose a school that serves their children best. But these programs come with a host of questions that are ripe for journalists to explore: What rules and regulations (or lack thereof) apply to private schools that receive public voucher money? What are the implications of religious school participation? Which families are benefiting most from these dollars, especially in places where vouchers are mostly paying for children who were already attending private school? What is the impact of vouchers on state budgets and existing public schools? In other jurisdictions, charter schools funded with public money have confronted opposition and scrutiny, with a lack of accountability and basic operating standards. This panel will look at this massive shift underway in K-12 education. Attendees will come away understanding the issue and its nuances and with specific stories they can report at home.

title: Mining the Epstein files
speakers: Julie K Brown; Khadeeja Safdar; Niala Boodhoo; Steve Eder
speaker_org_titles: Award-winning Washington, DC-based journalist, podcast and events host | Enterprise Reporter, The Wall Street Journal  | Investigative Reporter, The Miami Herald | Investigative Reporter, The New York Times
description_clean: 'The Epstein Files' landed early this year, making public a massive assortment of more than 3 million pages of records on the notorious predator. This session will explain how news organizations have mined the Epstein Files for stories. It will also discuss the many lessons learned from this unprecedented task and explore the array of challenges in reporting on such a huge set of records.

title: Lunch
Adjacent Event: Journalists Recovery Network
description_clean: The Journalists Recovery Network will meet and offer a sober space for journalists experiencing troubles with alcohol or substance use. This is an informal meeting for individuals who may struggle with substance use to meet our members and chat more about our organization. Feel free to reach out to us at journalistsrecoverynetwork@gmail.com.

title: New tools for this investigative moment
speakers: Ezana Ceman; Michael Wriston; Rowan Philp
speaker_org_titles: Co-Founder, Project Salt Box | Global Investigative Journalism Network | Product Manager for Data Platforms, Organized Crime and Corruption Reporting Project
description_clean: This panel will offer new practical tools from the US and around the world for urgent investigative needs. In this session, panelists will profile a tool that reduces the stakes for whistleblowers; the new Project Salt Box tracker to investigate migrant detention procurement; and updated OCCRP tools to investigate cross-border asset looting by authoritarians and their allies.

tracks: Data analysis; Hands on; Story ideas; Tools & tech
title: Google Sheets: Pivot tables
speaker_org_titles: Investigative Reporters and Editors
description_clean: Come to this hands-on class to learn how to use pivot tables in Google Sheets and make your data analysis faster and easier. This session is good for everyone who understands the basics of data journalism and has a beginner's knowledge of Google Sheets. Bring your computer!

title: Finding the story: the youth mental health crisis
speaker_org_titles: USA-Today Network Reporter
description_clean: School databases can provide a window into the youth mental health crisis, if reporters know how to analyze them. This hands-on session will show you how to use school data as the foundation for accountability reporting on student safety and mental health, including how to analyze statewide incident data, rank districts by incident categories and identify patterns, spikes and outliers that warrant deeper investigation. The session will also cover how to look beyond the numbers through public records requests, interviews with families and advocates and reporting on undercounting or misclassification of incidents. Attendees will leave with practical techniques to analyze state school datasets, identify districts where incidents are rising or disproportionately reported, and investigate potential systemic failures. This session is good for beginners. You must bring your own laptop (no tablets) to participate.

title: Introduction to R and RStudio
speaker_org_titles: Missouri School of Journalism
description_clean: Jump into data analysis with R, the powerful open-source programming language. In this class we’ll cover R fundamentals and learn our way around the RStudio interface for using R. This session is good for: People with a basic understanding of data analysis who are ready to go beyond spreadsheets. Attendees will need to bring their own laptop (no tablets).

title: How to make the most of your mentor/mentee relationship
Introduction to Python
speaker_org_titles: Director of technology, Investigative Reporters & Editors
description_clean: A free, three-hour crash course on how journalists are using the Python programming language to solve problems. We'll cover data analysis, web scraping and other common newsroom tasks. Participants will need a laptop (no tablets) and a free Google account.

title: 15 min break
Investigating the southern U.S. border
speaker_org_titles: New York Times
description_clean: Learn from panelists with experience reporting from the ground on immigration and the United States' southern border. The panelists will discuss the history of the border region, shifting immigration laws under Trump, and strategies for producing investigations about one of the most important regions of the US and the international geopolitical landscape.

title: Turning documents Into data with AI
speaker_org_titles: Senior Editor of Data Journalism at The Atlanta Journal-Constitution
description_clean: Investigative reporters drown in documents, from PDFs of court filings, inspection reports, and financial disclosures that contain structured information trapped in unstructured formats. In this session, we'll use AI tools to extract usable data from real-world documents and then build a simple, local web interface you can use to browse and search the results. No server or programming experience required — we'll walk through "vibecoding" the tool step by step using AI to write the code for us. You'll leave with a repeatable workflow for turning a stack of documents into a searchable dataset you can actually report from. Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

title: Under the Influence: Digging through federal lobbying records
speaker_org_titles: Reporter at NOTUS covering all things money in politics.
description_clean: The business of lobbying is big in Washington, and federal lobbyists are required to file periodic reports that offer reporters a window into the forces and characters working to influence policy and policymakers. Come learn how to access and use the Lobbying Disclosure Act and Foreign Agents Registration Act databases to explore the billions of dollars going toward influence peddling each year and how to build a story beyond the filings. Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

tracks: Beat reporting; Climate; Environmental; Reporting & writing strategies; Research; Story ideas
title: Climate disinformation reporting and databases
15 min break
How to build databases when none exist
No-code automations for investigations
speaker_org_titles: Missouri School of Journalism
description_clean: Learn how to use Zapier, a tool that helps you incorporate automation into daily workflows and newsgathering processes, no code necessary. You might be interested in automatically updating databases daily, or using automation to send out newsletters. Learn about uses for journalism and investigations. Attendees will need to bring their own laptops (preferably no tablets) for the training.

title: Detangling federal lobbying disclosures with LobbyR
speaker_org_titles: Bloomberg Industry Group
description_clean: Political influence is getting harder and harder to track, but the Lobbying Disclosure Act Reports helps cut through some of the noise. With LobbyR, researchers, journalists, and others can easily access, clean, and visualize a much more comprehensive version of federal lobbying disclosures to understand what companies, groups and other interests are asking Congress and the administration for. In this session, you’ll learn how just a few lines of R code will allow you to query, summarize, and plot real-world lobbying transparency data, enabling rapid insights and automated workflows. And there's no complex setup required. If you want to ask questions like “who lobbied on issue X in the last five years?”, or “how many dollars did registrant Y report?”, or “are there clients/registrants filing that overlap?”, LobbyR makes this work programmatic, reproducible, and transparent. Participants should have a basic knowledge of R. Attendees will need to bring their own laptop (no tablets). Participants should pre-register for an API key through this link: https://lda.gov/api/register/
